[A01] AGRICULTURE -- A01 includes inventions concerning agriculture, such as methods and apparatus for soil preparation, planting, cultivating, harvesting, and post-harvest handling. It also covers related technologies like irrigation, pest control, and farm machinery designed to improve crop production and efficiency.
[A01B] SOIL WORKING IN AGRICULTURE OR FORESTRY -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Making or covering furrows or holes for sowing, planting or manuring -> A01C5/00 - Mowers convertible to soil working apparatus or capable of soil working -> A01D42/04 - Mowers combined with soil working implements -> A01D43/12 - Soil working for engineering purposes -> E01 , E02 , E21  **Glossary:** - plant: any of a kingdom (Plantae) of multicellular eukaryotic mostly photosynthetic organisms typically lacking locomotive movement or obvious nervous or sensory organs and possessing cellulose cell walls
[A01B 1/00] Hand tools -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hand tools for gardening or for agricultural purposes.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Edge trimmers for lawns -> A01G3/06
[A01B 1/02] Spades -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hand shovels for gardening or agricultural purposes.
[A01B 1/022] Collapsible; extensible; combinations with other tools -- A01B 1/022 pertains to hand tools used for soil working in agriculture that are collapsible, extensible, or combined with other implements, such as fold-away shovels, telescopic spades or multi-function tools that can be reconfigured for different tasks.
[A01B 1/024] Foot protectors attached to the blade -- This subclass covers hand tools for soil working in which a protective device is mounted on the blade to shield the operator's foot from accidental contact. The foot guard is integral to the cutting or digging element, preventing injuries during agricultural or forestry tasks.
[A01B 1/026] with auxiliary handles for facilitating lifting -- A01B 1/026 covers hand tools used for soil working in agriculture or forestry that are equipped with auxiliary handles to make lifting easier. The auxiliary handles are added to improve ergonomics and reduce effort when the tool is raised or repositioned. This subclass falls under the broader categories of Human Necessities, Agriculture and Soil Working in Agriculture or Forestry.
[A01B 1/028] with ground abutment shoes or earth anchors for facilitating lifting -- A01B 1/028 covers hand tools used in soil working that incorporate ground-abutting shoes or earth-anchor devices to provide a stable base and aid in lifting the tool during operation, improving control and reducing user effort. This subclass falls under soil-working hand tools for agriculture and forestry.
[A01B 1/04] with teeth -- A01B 1/04 covers hand tools for soil working in agriculture or forestry that are equipped with teeth, such as toothed spades, cultivators or rakes. The teeth are used to cut, break up or lift soil and vegetation.
[A01B 1/06] Hoes -- A01B 1/06 refers to hand-held hoes used for manual soil working in agriculture or forestry, covering tools that dig, shape, or move soil such as pointed, flat, or scuffle-type hoes. This class groups the various designs of manual hoe implements.
[A01B 1/065] powered -- A01B 1/065 is a CPC subgroup that covers hand tools used for soil working in agriculture or forestry that are powered by an external energy source. It includes motor-driven cultivators, mechanical tillers, powered hoes and similar handheld equipment that assists planting, weeding or soil preparation.
[A01B 1/08] with a single blade -- A01B 1/08 designates hand tools for soil working that incorporate a single cutting blade, such as simple hoes or spades used in agriculture and forestry.
[A01B 1/10] with two or more blades -- A01B 1/10 designates hand tools for soil working in agriculture or forestry that incorporate two or more blades, covering implements such as multi-blade cultivators, weeders or plowshares used to cut, loosen or turn soil.
[A01B 11/00] Ploughs with oscillating, digging or piercing tools -- A01B 11/00 covers ploughs that employ oscillating, digging or piercing implements to turn or break soil. These devices use moving shares,
[A01B 1/12] with blades provided with teeth -- A01B 1/12 covers hand-operated soil-working tools that employ blades fitted with teeth, such as toothed cultivators, harrows or weeders used in agriculture and forestry. The teeth are designed to cut, break up, or lift soil and plant material.
[A01B 1/14] with teeth only -- A01B 1/14 covers hand-held soil-working tools that consist solely of teeth, such as simple cultivators, rakes or harrows where the working element is a row of teeth without additional blades or spikes. These devices are used to loosen, aerate or level soil in agricultural or forestry contexts.
[A01B 1/16] Tools for uprooting weeds -- The CPC subclass A01B 1/16 covers hand-operated implements designed to pull or extract unwanted plants from the soil. It includes tools such as weed pullers, specialized hoes, forks and similar devices used in agriculture, horticulture or forestry to uproot weeds efficiently.
[A01B 1/165] adapted for extracting a substantially cylindrical plug out of the earth -- A01B 1/165 covers hand tools specifically designed to pull a substantially cylindrical plug out of the ground, such as core-sampling devices or auger-type extractors used in soil working and forestry. The subclass addresses the geometry and extraction method of the plug rather than the drilling operation itself.
[A01B 1/18] Tong-like tools -- Tong-like implements are hand tools used in soil working for agriculture or forestry, such as tongs or similar grasping devices for handling soil, crops or debris. They are classified under CPC code A01B 1/18 within the Human Necessities-Agriculture-Soil Working hierarchy.
[A01B 1/20] Combinations of different kinds of hand tools -- The subclass A01B 1/20 includes hand tools that merge two or more distinct functions into a single implement for soil working, such as a hoe-fork, spade-rake or similar combined agricultural or forestry tools.
[A01B 1/22] Attaching the blades or the like to handles -- The class A01B 1/22 contains inventions relating to the means of joining cutting elements-blades, tines, or similar tools-to the handles of hand implements used for soil working in agriculture or forestry. It covers mechanical fasteners, clamps, rivets, screws, adhesives and interchangeable attachment systems that secure the blade to the handle.
[A01B 1/222] Adjustable blades -- A01B 1/222 covers hand tools used for soil working in agriculture or forestry that incorporate blades whose position, angle or width can be varied, such as adjustable hoes, cultivators or similar implements. It includes mechanisms that allow the user to modify the blade setting to suit different working conditions.
[A01B 1/225] around a substantially horizontal axis -- The subclass covers hand-operated soil-working implements whose functional element moves in
[A01B 1/227] Interchangeable blades -- A01B 1/227 covers hand tools for soil working in agriculture that use interchangeable blades, such as plows, cultivators and weeders, where the cutting or working element can be swapped or adjusted for different tasks or conditions. It includes the mounting, locking and adjustment mechanisms that allow rapid blade replacement.
[A01B 1/24] for treating meadows or lawns -- A01B 1/24 covers hand-operated implements used to treat grass-covered areas such as meadows, lawns or pasture, including mowing, raking, scarifying, aerating and spreading of seed, fertilizer or other treatments. The class is intended for tools that modify or maintain the surface of vegetated ground rather than large mechanised equipment. It falls under the broader categories of human necessities, agriculture, soil working in agriculture or forestry, and hand tools.
[A01B 1/243] for aerating -- A01B 1/243 covers hand tools designed to aerate soil, such as forks, spades or tines that create air channels to improve drainage and root growth. The devices are used in agriculture or forestry to loosen compacted earth without extensive digging. This subclass falls under soil-working hand tools within the broader human necessities category.
[A01B 1/246] for cutting sods or turves -- A01B 1/246 designates hand tools used for cutting sods or turves in soil-working operations. These devices are employed in agriculture and forestry to remove, transplant, or prepare sections of turf for planting or surface improvement.
[A01B 13/00] Ploughs or like machines for special purposes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Specially designed ploughs that perform, for example, a sidewards oscillating movement in addition to its linear displacement or ploughs containing tools or blades which perform some kind of additional discontinuous work on the soil. Further examples include ditch diggers, trench ploughs, forestry ploughs and ploughs for land or marsh reclamation.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Drainage device-laying apparatus -> E02B11/02
[A01B 13/02] for making or working ridges, e.g. with symmetrically arranged mouldboards -- A01B 13/02 refers to ploughs or similar machines specifically adapted to make or work ridges in the soil, commonly employing symmetrically arranged mouldboards. The code is used for equipment that forms ridged rows for cultivation, drainage or other agricultural purposes.
[A01B 13/025] with passively driven rotating disc-like elements for forming the ridge -- A01B 13/025 refers to ploughs or comparable soil-working machines that use passively driven, rotating disc-like elements to shape the soil into ridges. This design is employed in agriculture for creating raised beds or ridged rows without active power to the discs.
[A01B 13/04] for working in vineyards, orchards, or the like -- A01B 13/04 covers ploughs and similar soil-working machines specially designed for use in vineyards, orchards or comparable plantings. The subclass groups equipment that adapts conventional tillage functions to the narrow rows, slopes and delicate root systems typical of fruit-bearing crops. It sits within the hierarchy of Human Necessities → Agriculture → Soil Working in Agriculture or Forestry.
[A01B 13/06] Arrangements for preventing damage to the vines, or the like -- A01B 13/06 relates to protective arrangements that prevent damage to vines or similar crops during soil-working operations, such as shields, guards or other devices incorporated with ploughs or similar machines for special purposes. It is a subcategory of ploughs or like machines within agriculture.
[A01B 13/08] for working subsoil -- A01B 13/08 covers machines and implements designed specifically to work subsoil, such as specialised ploughs that dig beneath the surface for deep soil preparation or drainage. These devices are used in agriculture and forestry where conventional ploughing is insufficient for soil conditioning or infrastructure work.
[A01B 13/10] Special implements for lifting subsoil layers -- A01B 13/10 covers special implements designed to lift, turn or loosen subsoil layers rather than cut them, such as lifting blades, soil turners or similar attachments fitted to ploughs or comparable machines for agricultural or forestry work. These tools raise the soil to improve aeration, weed control or seed placement without deep excavation.
[A01B 13/12] Means for distributing the layers on the surface -- The A01B 13/12 subclass covers devices attached to ploughs or similar agricultural machines that spread or distribute material in layers over the soil surface, such as mulch, compost, or fertilizer. It includes mechanisms that ensure an even coating of the surface during special-purpose soil working.
[A01B 13/14] for working soil in two or more layers -- The A01B 13/14 subgroup includes ploughs or similar agricultural machines that till the soil in two or more layers at the same time, enabling simultaneous working of multiple depths. It is used for soil preparation in farming and forestry where layered cultivation is required.
[A01B 13/16] Machines for combating erosion, e.g. basin-diggers, furrow-dammers -- A01B 13/16 covers machines designed to prevent soil erosion, such as basin-diggers and furrow-dammers used in agricultural soil working. These devices belong to the plough and related equipment category, focusing on erosion control during tillage.
[A01B 15/00] Elements, tools, or details of ploughs -- This CPC subclass covers the components, accessories and design details of ploughs used for soil preparation in agriculture and forestry. It includes implements such as mouldboards, share attachments, depth control mechanisms and other structural parts.
[A01B 15/02] Plough blades -- A01B 15/02 designates the blades of ploughs, the cutting or working elements that engage the soil to turn it during cultivation. These components are essential parts of tillage equipment used in agriculture and forestry.
[A01B 15/025] specially adapted for working subsoil -- A01B 15/025 covers implements that are specially adapted for working the subsoil, such as deep-ploughing tools, subsoilers and other devices designed to break up compacted layers below the normal ploughing depth. These elements often feature stronger frames, adjustable angles or deeper cutting sections to improve soil aeration and root penetration.
[A01B 15/04] Shares -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details about the subsoiler's or mouldboard's blades
[A01B 15/06] Interchangeable or adjustable shares -- A01B 15/06 covers interchangeable or adjustable shares of ploughs, meaning the removable or modifiable components that form the cutting surface of a plough. These shares can be swapped or set to different positions to suit varying soil conditions, crops, or working depths.
[A01B 15/08] Mouldboards -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details about the subsoiler's or mouldboard's blades
[A01B 15/10] Interchangeable or adjustable mouldboards -- A01B 15/10 relates to ploughs whose mouldboards can be interchanged or adjusted to suit different soil conditions or tillage requirements. It covers designs that allow the mouldboard to be swapped or its angle altered without replacing the entire implement.
[A01B 15/12] Beams -- A01B 15/12 covers beams used in ploughs, detailing the structural members that form part of the plough frame or attach to it. It falls under the soil-working category of agriculture, encompassing tools and components that enable the preparation of soil in farming or forestry.
[A01B 15/125] Articulated beams -- A01B 15/125 covers articulated beams, the hinged or jointed components that connect the ploughshare to the rest of a plough and allow it to follow the contour of the ground. These elements are part of the tools and details of ploughs used in soil working for agriculture and forestry.

[A01B 15/145] the plough blades being connected to the plough beam for unisono adjustment of the furrow width -- The subclass describes arrangements in which the plough blades are linked to the plough-beam so that they can be moved together for a single adjustment of the furrow width.
[A01B 15/16] Discs -- A01B 15/16 pertains to disc components employed in ploughs for soil working, including disc-type ploughs or disc harrows that cut, lift and turn earth. The entry covers the design, arrangement and functional details of these circular cutting elements used in agricultural tillage.
[A01B 15/18] Coulters -- Coulters are blades attached to a plough that cut through soil and vegetation before the main share engages, reducing draft force and improving seed placement. They are especially useful in heavy or coarse soils to minimise disturbance.
[A01B 15/20] Special adjusting means for tools of ploughs drawn by, or mounted on tractors working on hillsides or slopes -- A01B 15/20 covers devices that adjust plough tools mounted on or pulled by tractors when working on hillsides or slopes. The classification pertains to specialized mechanisms that compensate for terrain incline to maintain proper depth and angle of the implements during soil cultivation. It falls under the broader categories of human necessities, agriculture, and soil working in agriculture or forestry.
[A01B 17/00] Ploughs with special additional arrangements, e.g. means for putting manure under the soil, clod-crushers -- This subclass covers ploughs equipped with auxiliary devices that place manure or other soil amendments under the soil or crush clods during tillage, improving soil conditioning and weed control.
[A01B 17/002] Means for putting manure, debris, straw or the like under the soil  to plough-in weeds  A01B39/18 -- This subclass covers devices built into ploughs that place manure, straw, debris or similar material beneath the soil as the implement advances, usually to suppress weeds or enrich the ground. It includes mechanisms that feed, spread or inject these substances during ploughing.
[A01B 17/004] Clod-crushers -- The A01B 17/004 class covers agricultural ploughs that incorporate clod-crushing mechanisms to break up soil lumps during cultivation, often combined with features such as integrated manure placement.
[A01B 17/006] with driven tools -- A01B 17/006 covers ploughs that incorporate driven tools for special functions such as inserting manure beneath the soil, crushing clods, or other additional arrangements that enhance soil working in agriculture or forestry. It falls under the broader categories of human necessities, agriculture, and soil working in agriculture or forestry.
[A01B 17/008] rotating about a vertical or steeply inclined axis -- A01B 17/008 covers ploughs that rotate about a vertical or steeply inclined axis, enabling special functions such as placing manure beneath the soil or crushing clods while tilling. These machines belong to the soil-working group within agriculture and are designed for enhanced soil conditioning.
[A01B 19/00] Harrows -- A01B 19/00 defines harrows, agricultural tools that break up, smooth and level soil after plowing, removing clods and preparing a fine seedbed for planting. They are used in soil-working operations within the broader field of agriculture.
[A01B 19/02] with tools rigidly or elastically attached to a tool-frame -- A01B 19/02 refers to harrow implements whose working tools are mounted to a frame either rigidly or with an elastic connection, allowing the tools to stay in position while providing limited movement or vibration damping. This subclass includes designs where the attachment method-fixed or spring-loaded-affects soil penetration and seed-bed preparation.
[A01B 19/04] with two or more tool-frames -- A01B 19/04 covers harrows that incorporate two or more tool-frames, used for soil working in agriculture or forestry.
[A01B 19/06] with tools or tool-frames moved to-and-fro mechanically -- A01B 19/06 covers harrows in which the working tools or their frames are driven to move repeatedly back and forth by a mechanical power source, such as a motor or engine, rather than manually. This subcategory includes designs that automate the to-and-fro motion for soil conditioning or weed control.
[A01B 19/08] with link network supporting tooth-like tools -- A01B 19/08 covers harrows whose tooth-like tools are mounted on a linking network that flexibly connects the teeth, allowing individual movement while maintaining overall alignment. The linked arrangement improves soil penetration, reduces clogging and provides more uniform soil working in agricultural or forestry settings.
[A01B 19/10] Lifting or cleaning apparatus -- A01B 19/10 covers devices for lifting, raising or cleaning harrows used in soil working, such as mechanisms that detach, elevate, or remove soil and debris from the tines. These apparatuses facilitate maintenance and improve the efficiency of harrowing operations in agriculture and forestry.
[A01B 21/00] Harrows with rotary non-driven tools -- A01B 21/00 covers harrows that use rotary tools which are not driven by a separate power source, relying on the forward motion of the implement to spin the tools for breaking up soil, leveling ground, or removing weeds. These devices are used in agriculture and forestry for basic soil preparation.
[A01B 21/02] with tooth-like tools -- A01B 21/02 covers harrows that employ rotary, non-driven tools fitted with tooth-like elements for soil working. The classification applies to equipment that mechanically disturbs the ground using toothed implements that break up clods and level the surface without motor-driven rotation.
[A01B 21/04] on horizontally-arranged axles -- This subgroup covers harrows that use rotary, non-driven tools mounted on axles arranged horizontally across the implement. It includes designs where the rotating components are fixed in position and powered by the forward motion of the machine rather than an external drive.
[A01B 21/06] on vertically-arranged axles -- A01B 21/06 classifies harrows equipped with rotary, non-driven tools that are mounted on vertically-arranged axles, allowing multiple rows of implements to operate at different heights. This design is used in soil-working equipment for agriculture, falling under the broader categories of human necessities, agriculture and soil working.

[A01B 21/083] of the type comprising four individual disk groups each pivotally connected at fixed locations to a substantially rigid frame -- The Cooperative Patent Classification entry A01B 21/083 pertains to harrows with rotary non-driven tools, specifically those of a design featuring four individual disk groups that are each pivotally attached at fixed points on a rigid frame. This classification is used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office for organizing patents related to agricultural soil working equipment.
[A01B 21/086] of the type in which the disc-like tools are individually mounted -- A01B 21/086 covers harrows used in soil working that employ rotary, non-driven tools whose disc-like implements are mounted separately rather than as a continuous band. This design allows each disc to rotate independently while being pulled through the ground, providing flexible cultivation of soil in agricultural or forestry operations.
[A01B 23/00] Elements, tools, or details of harrows -- A01B 23/00 covers the various components, attachments and design details of harrows, the implements used to break up, level or smooth soil in agricultural and forestry operations. It includes elements such as tines, frames, wheels, adjusters and other auxiliary parts that affect the performance or adjustability of the harrow.
[A01B 23/02] Teeth -- The CPC subclass A01B 23/02, under Human Necessities → Agriculture → Soil Working in Agriculture or Forestry → Elements, tools, or details of harrows, covers the design, shape, arrangement and material of the teeth fitted to harrows. It includes specifications of tooth geometry, spacing, attachment methods and any variations intended to improve soil cutting or seedbed preparation.
[A01B 23/04] Frames -- The CPC subclass A01B 23/04 covers the structural frames that form the main supporting elements of harrows used for soil working in agriculture or forestry. It includes designs, configurations and construction details of these frames, which may be metal, wood or composite, and defines how they connect to other harrow components.
[A01B 23/043] specially adapted for harrows with non-rotating tools -- A01B 23/043 is a subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification that covers harrows specially adapted to use non-rotating tools, focusing on fixed implements for soil working. It falls under the hierarchy Human Necessities → Agriculture → Soil Working in Agriculture or Forestry.
[A01B 23/046] Specially adapted for harrows with rotating tools -- A01B 23/046 covers harrows whose working tools are specially adapted to rotate during operation, including gear-driven, motorized or other mechanisms that cause the implements to spin for improved soil loosening, weed control or seedbed preparation.
[A01B 23/06] Discs -- A01B 23/06 refers to the disc elements of harrow tools used in soil-working operations. It covers the design, construction and mounting details of the circular cutting or smoothing discs that break up and level ground in agricultural or forestry settings.
[A01B 25/00] Harrows with special additional arrangements, e.g. means for distributing fertilisers -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Machines specially adapted for working soil on which crops are growing -> A01B39/00
[A01B 27/00] Other machines for working soil -- A01B 27/00 groups machines that work soil but are not covered by more specific subclasses such as ploughs or cultivators. It includes equipment for loosening, mixing, aerating or otherwise treating soil in agricultural or forestry contexts, providing general soil-working functions. This subclass sits under Human Necessities → Agriculture → Soil working in agriculture or forestry.
[A01B 27/005] comprising roller-type tools moving on the ground -- A01B 27/005 pertains to agricultural machines that incorporate roller-type tools which travel along the ground during operation, such as rollers used for soil compaction, smoothing or conditioning. The subclass covers any device where the rollers are integral to the implement and move while working the soil.
[A01B 29/00] Rollers -- Rollers are agricultural tools designed to compact, level or break up soil during field preparation, often mounted on tractors or combined with other implements. They are used to improve seedbed uniformity, increase soil density and facilitate subsequent planting operations.
[A01B 29/02] with smooth surface -- A01B 29/02 covers agricultural rollers that have a smooth surface, used for soil working such as leveling, compaction or seedbed preparation in farming and forestry. The code falls under the hierarchy HUMAN NECESSITIES > AGRICULTURE > SOIL WORKING IN AGRICULTURE OR FORESTRY. This subclassifies rollers without ridges, tines or patterned surfaces.
[A01B 29/04] with non-smooth surface formed of rotatably-mounted rings or discs or with projections or ribs on the roller body -- A01B 29/04 covers agricultural rollers whose
[A01B 29/041] of "Cambridge"-type, i.e. the soil-pressing rings being stacked on a shaft -- A01B 29/041 covers rollers, specifically Cambridge-type soil-pressing rings that are stacked on a shaft to compact soil in agricultural or forestry operations.
[A01B 29/043] Tyre-packers -- It covers agricultural implements that combine a tyre and a roller to compact or pack soil, typically used after seeding or cultivation. This subgroup falls within the rollers class of soil-working equipment for agriculture and forestry.
[A01B 29/045] the soil-pressing body being a drum -- A01B 29/045 designates soil-pressing bodies that are drums, i.e., cylindrical rollers used to compact or smooth soil in agricultural or forestry operations. It covers drum-type rollers employed for soil working such as plowing, harrowing, or seedbed preparation.
[A01B 29/046] the soil-pressing body being a helical coil -- This subclass covers agricultural implements whose soil-pressing body is a helical coil, used to compact soil during tillage or seed-bed preparation. It is a specific type of roller in soil-working equipment.
[A01B 29/048] Bar cage rollers -- Bar cage rollers are cylindrical implements fitted with a cage of parallel bars that support the roller and enhance grip on the ground. They are used on agricultural and forestry soil-working tools such as subsoilers, cultivators and other implements to compact soil and prevent slippage during operation.
[A01B 29/06] with special additional arrangements -- A01B 29/06 refers to agricultural rollers that incorporate special additional arrangements, such as adjustable pressure devices, auxiliary mechanisms or supplementary features designed to improve soil compaction, leveling or seed-bed preparation during soil-working operations.
[A01B 3/00] Ploughs -- A01B 3/00 covers ploughs, devices used to turn, cut or lift soil in agricultural or forestry operations. It includes various designs such as mouldboard, disc and chisel ploughs intended to prepare ground for planting or improve soil structure.
[A01B 3/02] Man-driven ploughs -- A01B 3/02 covers ploughs that are operated manually rather than by animals or machines, including hand-held or foot-driven implements used to turn soil for planting. These tools are classified under human necessities, agriculture, and soil working, reflecting their role in basic, low-tech cultivation practices.
[A01B 3/04] Animal-drawn ploughs -- The subclass contains ploughs that are pulled by animals such as horses, oxen or mules. It includes designs where animal-provided traction drives the earth-working implement, covering various share shapes, frame constructions and hitching arrangements.
[A01B 3/06] without alternating possibility, i.e. incapable of making an adjacent furrow on return journey -- A01B 3/06 classifies ploughs that lack an alternating mechanism, meaning they cannot cut an adjacent furrow on the return stroke and therefore work only one furrow per pass. The entry applies to simple single-furrow designs without the capability to make alternating furrows.
[A01B 3/08] Swing ploughs -- A01B 3/08 covers swing ploughs, which are ploughs whose frame or share pivots about a vertical axis to turn the soil, allowing shallow to medium-depth cultivation. These implements can be drawn by animals or machines and are suited for small fields or garden plots.
[A01B 3/10] Trussed-beam ploughs -- A01B 3/10 covers ploughs in which the main share or blade is supported by a trussed-beam frame, giving the tool rigidity and reducing bending while turning soil. These designs are used in agriculture for efficient soil working and fall under the broader category of soil-working equipment.
[A01B 31/00] Drags -- A01B 31/00, titled "Drags", covers agricultural implements that are pulled through the soil to move, loosen, level or otherwise prepare it, such as soil draggers, harrows and similar drag-type tools used in farming and forestry soil work.
[A01B 3/12] Two-wheel beam ploughs -- A01B 3/12 covers ploughs that use a beam with two
[A01B 3/14] Frame ploughs -- A01B 3/14 covers frame ploughs, a type of agricultural implement where the share and moldboard are mounted on a rigid frame that is drawn through the soil. These ploughs provide a stable structure for cutting and turning soil in conventional tillage operations.
[A01B 3/16] Alternating ploughs, i.e. capable of making an adjacent furrow on return journey -- Alternating ploughs are implements that cut a furrow on the forward pass and then cut a second, adjacent furrow on the return pass, allowing two rows per travel cycle and improving field efficiency. They fall under CPC A01B 3/16, a subgroup of ploughs within soil-working equipment for agriculture.
[A01B 3/18] Turn-wrest ploughs -- Turn-wrest ploughs are implements that incorporate a turning or swiveling share to break soil while the plough is drawn, allowing the furrow to be turned as the share penetrates. They are used for furrow turning in soil preparation.
[A01B 3/20] Balance ploughs -- Balance ploughs are a type of plough where the ploughshare is supported by a balance beam that distributes the load between the wheels, allowing lighter draft animals to pull it. They are used for turning over soil in fields and are classified under A01B 3/20 in the CPC.
[A01B 3/22] with parallel plough units used alternately -- A01B 3/22 classifies ploughs that employ multiple parallel plough units which are used alternately rather than simultaneously, allowing successive operation of the units to improve soil turnover and reduce draft force. This design is typical for systems where alternating action enhances efficiency on various soils.
[A01B 3/24] Tractor-drawn ploughs -- Tractor-drawn ploughs are agricultural implements designed to be pulled by a tractor for primary soil preparation, including moldboard, disc, chisel, and similar types that cut, turn, or break up the ground in fields and orchards. Their use replaces animal or manual power, allowing greater speed and efficiency in tillage operations.
[A01B 3/26] without alternating possibility -- A01B 3/26 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to ploughs that do not offer an alternating operation, meaning they perform a single type of soil working action without the capability to switch between different modes or configurations.
[A01B 3/28] Alternating ploughs -- Alternating ploughs are agricultural implements that use a pair of working units arranged so that each operates on opposite sides of the furrow in succession, turning the soil in alternating directions to improve aeration and reduce wear on the tool. This design falls under the A01B 3/28 subclass of the CPC for ploughs.
[A01B 3/30] Turn-wrest ploughs -- Turn-wrest ploughs are a class of ploughs that cut the soil with a share and then overturn the cut slice, burying weeds and residues while exposing fresh soil for planting. They belong to the A01B 3/30 subgroup of ploughs within the soil-working section of agricultural classifications.
[A01B 33/00] Tilling implements with rotary driven tools -- A01B 33/00 covers agricultural tools for soil preparation that use rotary-driven implements such as rototillers, sub-soilers and similar devices. These machines loosen, break up, and mix the soil by means of a powered rotating blade or disc, and are intended for tillage in crop production or land-management activities.
[A01B 33/02] with tools on horizontal shaft transverse to direction of travel -- A01B 33/02 covers tillage tools that are mounted on a horizontal shaft positioned across the machine, with the shaft running transverse to the direction of travel. The classification applies to implements that use such transverse-driven rotary tools for soil cultivation, such as certain rototillers and cultivators.
[A01B 33/021] with rigid tools -- A01B 33/021 addresses tillage implements that use rigid rotary-driven tools such as fixed blades, discs or bars to work the soil. It classifies equipment where the rotating elements are non-flexible, separating them from devices with flexible or spring-loaded tools used in soil preparation.
[A01B 33/022] with helicoidal tools -- This subclass covers rotary tillage implements that employ helicoidal tools such as helical blades, screws or auger-type elements to stir and loosen soil. The devices are designed for agricultural or forestry soil working, falling under the broader group of tillage
[A01B 33/024] with disk-like tools -- This subclass pertains to rotary tillage implements that use disk-shaped tools, such as disks or disk-like cutters, to break up and prepare soil in agriculture or forestry. It falls under the broader category of soil-working implements with rotary driven tools.
[A01B 33/025] with spring tools, i.e. resiliently- or flexibly-attached rigid tools -- A01B 33/025 classifies rotary tillage implements whose rigid working tools are mounted on springs or other flexible connections, allowing the tools to yield under load. This arrangement improves soil penetration and reduces shock by letting the tools move resiliently while the implement rotates.
[A01B 33/027] Operator supported tools, e.g. using a harness for supporting the tool or power unit -- This subclass covers tillage implements with rotary driven tools that are carried or supported on the operator, typically using a harness or other wearable system that holds the tool or its power unit on the user's body for hands-free operation.
[A01B 33/028] of the walk-behind type  walk-type tractors  B62D51/04 -- A01B 33/028 classifies tilling implements with rotary driven tools, specifically those of the walk-behind type or walk-type tractors. This classification also intersects with B62D51/04, which pertains to steering arrangements for such machines.
[A01B 33/04] with tools on horizontal shaft parallel to direction of travel -- This CPC subcategory covers agricultural tillage implements that use rotary tools mounted on a horizontal shaft running parallel to the direction of travel, such as cultivators or ploughs where the working elements are aligned with the forward motion of the machine.
[A01B 33/06] with tools on vertical or steeply-inclined shaft -- A01B 33/06 covers tillage implements whose rotary driven tools are mounted on a shaft that is vertical or steeply inclined, allowing the soil-working elements to engage the ground from a near-upright orientation. This subgroup applies to equipment such as vertical or steep-shaft rotors used for loosening, mixing or aerating soil in agricultural or forestry operations.
[A01B 33/065] comprising a plurality of rotors carried by an elongate, substantially closed transmission casing, transversely connectable to a tractor -- The code A01B 33/065 covers tillage implements that have several rotors mounted inside an elongated, essentially closed transmission housing, which can be connected sideways to a tractor. The design provides power transmission to the rotors while protecting the mechanism from soil and debris.
[A01B 33/08] Tools -- A01B 33/08 refers to the tools that form part of rotary-driven tillage implements, such as the cutting, digging or soil-mixing components mounted on rotors. It covers the physical elements that engage the ground to loosen, aerate or incorporate organic material, distinguishing them from the power-transmission or frame parts of the machine.
[A01B 33/082] Transmissions; Gearings; Power distribution -- A01B 33/082 covers the transmission and gearing arrangements used in rotary-driven tillage tools, detailing how power is distributed from the drive source to the working components of soil-working implements. This subclass groups mechanisms such as gear trains, shafts, belts and hydraulic drives that convert engine output into the rotational motion required for effective tillage. It falls within the broader CPC hierarchy for agricultural soil-working equipment.
[A01B 33/085] specially adapted for tools on a vertical shaft -- A01B 33/085 covers tillage equipment that uses rotary‐driven tools mounted on a vertical shaft, specially adapted for such vertical-shaft configurations. The classification falls under soil working in agriculture, focusing on implements where the rotating tools are oriented vertically to improve soil penetration and mixing.
[A01B 33/087] Lifting devices; Depth regulation devices; Mountings -- This subclass deals with devices used on rotary tillage tools to raise or lower the implement, control the working depth, or provide mounting structures. It includes hydraulic or mechanical lifts, depth-regulating rods, brackets and other fittings that attach the tool to a tractor or other drive unit.
[A01B 33/10] Structural or functional features of the tools -- A01B 33/10 covers the structural and functional characteristics of rotary-driven tillage tools, such as rotors, blades, discs and their mounting, geometry, materials and power-transmission features that influence soil engagement, durability and performance.
[A01B 33/103] the rotating shaft being oriented horizontally -- A01B 33/103 refers to tillage tools that use a rotary driven implement whose shaft is arranged horizontally, typical of horizontal-axis rotary cultivators or rototillers. This subclass groups devices where the rotation axis runs parallel to the ground to break up soil or prepare seedbeds. It falls under soil-working implements with rotary driven tools within the broader agriculture hierarchy.
[A01B 33/106] the rotating shaft being oriented vertically or steeply inclined -- A01B 33/106 relates to rotary tillage tools in which the driven shaft is positioned vertically or at a steep inclination, such as in tillage implements that use a rotating blade or disc to break up soil. This subclass falls under soil-working equipment for agriculture, specifically tilling implements with rotary driven tools.
[A01B 33/12] Arrangement of the tools -- A01B 33/12 covers the arrangement of rotary-driven tools on tillage implements used for soil working in agriculture or forestry. It addresses the spatial configuration, mounting and relative positioning of the tools to achieve effective soil disruption.
[A01B 33/125] screening of tools rotating about a vertical shaft -- A01B 33/125 covers screening devices attached to tillage implements that rotate around a vertical shaft to separate soil, rocks or debris from the cultivated ground. It falls under soil-working tools with rotary driven implements, used in agriculture and forestry for preparing seedbeds.
[A01B 33/14] Attaching the tools to the rotating shaft, e.g. resiliently -attached tools -- A01B 33/14 covers the means of connecting rotary implements to a driven shaft, focusing on attachment methods such as resilient or flexible mounts, quick-change clamps, and other mechanisms that allow the tools to be secured while absorbing vibrations or accommodating misalignment.
[A01B 33/142] the rotating shaft being oriented horizontally -- A01B 33/142 refers to tilting implements with rotary driven tools in which the rotating shaft is positioned horizontally. This arrangement is used in soil-working equipment such as rotary cultivators or rotary ploughs to
[A01B 33/144] with spring tools or resiliently-attached rigid tools -- The A01B 33/144 subclass concerns rotary-driven tillage tools that incorporate spring-loaded or resiliently-mounted rigid implements, allowing the tools to flex or bounce while working the soil. This design improves soil contact, reduces shock, and enhances durability of cultivators and rotary tillers.
[A01B 33/146] the rotating shaft being oriented vertically or steeply inclined -- A01B 33/146 refers to tillage implements equipped with rotary-driven tools whose rotating shaft is positioned vertically or at a steep incline. [A01B 33/148] with spring tools or resiliently-attached rigid tools -- A01B 33/148 covers tillage implements whose rotary-driven tools are mounted on springs or other resilient attachments, allowing the tools to flex or bounce during operation. This design reduces shock and maintains consistent soil contact, improving efficiency in soil preparation.
[A01B 33/16] with special additional arrangements -- A01B 33/16 refers to tillage implements that use rotary-driven tools and incorporate special additional arrangements, such as adjustable depth controls, variable-speed drives, auxiliary cutters or other auxiliary mechanisms that enhance the basic rotary tillage function. This subclass falls under soil-working equipment within the broader agriculture category.
[A01B 3/32] Balance ploughs -- Balance ploughs are a type of plough that use a balanced lever or weight system to control the depth and orientation of the share, allowing adjustable soil turning while reducing draft force. They are employed in agricultural or forestry soil working to improve efficiency across varying soil conditions.
[A01B 3/34] with parallel plough units used alternately -- This subclass covers ploughs with parallel plough units arranged side-by-side that are used alternately, so one unit works while another returns, increasing efficiency. It belongs to CPC group A01B for soil-working equipment in agriculture.
[A01B 3/36] Ploughs mounted on tractors -- A01B 3/36 covers ploughs that are directly attached to or integrated with tractors, where the tractor supplies power and steering for the implement. These tractor-mounted ploughs are used for primary soil preparation in agriculture, replacing traditional draft-animal or standalone machines.
[A01B 3/38] without alternating possibility -- A01B 3/38 refers to ploughs that lack an alternating mechanism, meaning the working unit is fixed in one position and cannot be shifted to the opposite side during operation. Such devices work continuously in a single orientation without a system for changing the direction of the furrow.
[A01B 3/40] Alternating ploughs -- Alternating ploughs are implements that create adjacent furrows by shifting the working position of the furrow opener between successive passes, allowing one set of moldboards to work while the other remains idle, which reduces soil disturbance and improves efficiency.
[A01B 3/42] Turn-wrest ploughs -- Turn-wrest ploughs are a family of ploughs in which the share or cutting blade is rotated into a vertical position during operation and then pulled out, or wrested, to break and turn soil, enabling efficient land preparation for planting.
[A01B 3/421] with a headstock frame made in one piece -- The subclass A01B 3/421 refers to ploughs whose headstock frame is manufactured as a single, integral piece rather than being assembled from separate parts, encompassing designs where the headstock-including the connection between the share and drawbar-is cast, forged or molded as one unit to enhance strength and simplify construction.
[A01B 3/4215] the headstock being provided with two or more hydraulic cylinders -- The entry covers plough headstocks equipped with two-or-more hydraulic cylinders that control the vertical movement of the cutting share and other working parts. These cylinders provide synchronized lift and depth control, improving efficiency and adaptability in soil working.

[A01B 3/44] with parallel plough units used alternately -- This subclass covers ploughs that employ two or more parallel plough units arranged side-by-side and operated alternately, allowing one unit to cut while the other follows, which improves efficiency and soil turnover in agricultural or forestry tillage.
[A01B 3/46] Ploughs supported partly by tractor and partly by their own wheels -- A01B 3/46 covers ploughs that receive power partly from a tractor and partly from wheels attached to the implement itself, allowing the machine to move under its own traction while still being towed. This subcategory falls under soil-working equipment within the agriculture section of the CPC.
[A01B 3/462] Alternating ploughs with frame rotating about a vertical axis -- A01B 3/462 covers alternating ploughs whose working frame pivots around a vertical axis to swing the share from one side of the furrow to the other, producing successive cuts without reversing the machine. This design enables continuous forward motion while alternating the direction of soil displacement, improving efficiency in row-by-row cultivation.
[A01B 3/464] Alternating ploughs with frame rotating about a horizontal axis, e.g. turn-wrest ploughs -- This CPC subclass A01B 3/464 covers ploughs whose working frame swings alternately about a horizontal axis, commonly called turn-wrest ploughs, used to turn soil in rows. It includes designs where the frame rotates to lift and invert the soil during forward travel.
[A01B 3/466] the frame comprising an articulated beam -- A01B 3/466 covers plough frames that incorporate an articulated beam, meaning the beam is jointed or hinged to allow relative movement between its sections. This design enables the plough to follow the contour of the ground and adjust depth more easily, improving soil turnover on uneven terrain.
[A01B 3/468] the plough wheels being actively driven -- This classification covers ploughs whose wheels are actively driven rather than free-rolling, describing mechanisms that provide propulsion and improve soil turnover. It falls under soil working in agriculture, specifically ploughs, and is used to classify inventions that incorporate motor-driven wheel systems.
[A01B 3/50] Self-propelled ploughs -- A01B 3/50 covers self-propelled ploughs, agricultural machines that move under their own power to prepare soil. These implements combine a ploughshare with built-in traction, such as wheels or tracks, eliminating the need for external towing.

[A01B 35/30] Undercarriages -- Undercarriages are components or complete assemblies attached beneath agricultural or forestry machines that support, stabilize, or guide the vehicle while it works the soil. [A01B 3/54] without alternating possibility -- A01B 3/54 designates ploughs that are built to operate only in one fixed mode, lacking any mechanism to alternate their working position or direction. These devices cannot be switched between forward and reverse or between different furrowing configurations, offering a single, non-adjustable ploughing action.
[A01B 3/56] Alternating ploughs -- Alternating ploughs are implements that perform successive, opposite-direction soil turning with each pass, allowing a single set of mouldboards to work both sides of a furrow. This design reduces mechanical complexity and improves efficiency in conventional tillage operations.
[A01B 3/58] with two wheels -- A01B 3/58 designates ploughs that are fitted with two wheels for soil preparation in agriculture or forestry. It is a subcategory of ploughs within the Cooperative Patent Classification hierarchy that runs from human necessities through agriculture to soil working.
[A01B 3/60] Alternating ploughs -- Alternating ploughs are implements in which successive plough shares are arranged to work in opposite directions or at staggered intervals, providing more uniform soil turnover and reducing clogging. They are used in agriculture to improve the efficiency of soil preparation on various terrains.
[A01B 3/62] Balance ploughs -- Balance ploughs are agricultural implements that use a balanced beam or lever to transmit the draft force from the draft animal or tractor to the share, allowing the plough to be lifted and lowered without changing the angle of the share. They are commonly employed for shallow tillage of light soils and can be adjusted for depth by moving the balance point.
[A01B 3/64] Cable ploughs -- Cable ploughs are agricultural implements that use a flexible cable or pulling element instead of a rigid beam to drag the ploughshare through the soil. They are employed for shallow tillage, especially in light soils or on sloping ground where conventional ploughs are difficult to maneuver. The design allows better control and reduced soil disturbance.
[A01B 3/66] with motor-driven winding apparatus mounted on the plough -- A01B 3/66 designates ploughs equipped with a motor-driven winding mechanism that is mounted directly on the plough, enabling automatic cable or rope handling for implements or accessories. This subclass falls under soil-working equipment in agriculture, specifically the plough category.
[A01B 3/68] Cable systems with one or two engines -- A01B 3/68 covers cable-driven ploughing equipment that uses a single or twin-engine arrangement to pull cables which move the implement through the soil. The classification includes systems where the cables are wound on drums or reels, providing traction for soil working in agricultural or forestry settings.
[A01B 3/70] Systems with one engine for working uphill -- This subgroup covers ploughing machines that use a single power source to pull the implement up a slope, describing configurations where one engine provides traction and draft for uphill operation. It includes designs for tractors or self-propelled ploughs adapted to inclines, emphasizing hill-climbing capability within soil-working equipment.
[A01B 37/00] Devices for loosening soil compacted by wheels or the like -- A01B 37/00 covers devices that break up soil that has become compacted by vehicle wheels, tracks or similar loads, improving aeration and water infiltration. It applies to any means that prevents cable slack or displacement while the plough works in the soil.

It addresses the integration of electric drive technology into ploughing equipment for improved efficiency and reduced reliance on fossil-fuel engines.

[A01B 49/022] at least one tool being actively driven -- A01B 49/022 covers combined soil-working machines used in agriculture or forestry that incorporate at least one tool that is powered (actively driven) rather than passive, such as a tractor-mounted implement with a motor-driven tillage or cultivation element. This subclass distinguishes machines where the tool's motion is supplied by an onboard or external power source.
[A01B 49/025] about a substantially vertical axis -- This CPC subclass concerns combined agricultural machines that employ a working element positioned on a substantially vertical axis, such as vertical-axis rotors or mixers used for soil working, mixing or related operations in farming and forestry.
[A01B 49/027] with a rotating, soil working support element, e.g. a roller -- It covers agricultural combined machines that incorporate a rotating support element such as a roller to improve soil treatment, often used in tillage or seedbed preparation. The rotating element assists in smoothing or consolidating soil after primary working.
[A01B 49/04] Combinations of soil-working tools with non-soil-working tools, e.g. planting tools -- A01B 49/04 covers combined machines that integrate soil-working implements such as ploughs or tillers with non-soil-working tools, for example planting devices. These combinations allow simultaneous soil preparation and seed placement, improving efficiency
[A01B 49/06] for sowing or fertilising -- A01B 49/06 refers to combined machines used in soil working for agriculture or forestry that perform sowing and fertilising in a single unit. These devices integrate seed placement and fertilizer distribution to streamline field operations.
[A01B 49/065] the soil-working tools being actively driven -- A01B 49/065 covers combined agricultural machines in which the soil-working implements are actively driven by the machine's own power source, such as engine- or motor-powered tillers, cultivators or ploughs that operate without being pulled or pushed.
[A01B 5/00] Ploughs with rolling non-driven tools, e.g. discs -- This CPC subclass A01B 5/00 covers ploughs that incorporate rolling non-driven tools such as discs, used to break and turn soil while the rolling elements rotate freely rather than being powered. It
[A01B 5/02] drawn by animals -- The A01B 5/02 subclass includes animal-drawn ploughs that use rolling, non-driven implements such as discs to turn and break up soil. These devices are part of the broader soil-working category in agriculture, focusing on simple, draft-animal powered equipment for preparing fields.
[A01B 5/04] drawn by tractors -- A01B 5/04 refers to ploughs that incorporate rolling, non-driven tools such as discs and are pulled by tractors for soil working, encompassing implements used in agriculture to turn or break up the ground.
[A01B 5/06] without alternating possibility -- A01B 5/06 covers ploughs equipped with rolling, non-driven implements such as disc wheels that cannot be reversed or alternated during operation.
[A01B 5/08] Alternating ploughs -- Alternating ploughs are soil-working implements that use a series of non-driven rolling tools, such as discs,
[A01B 5/10] mounted or partly-mounted on tractors -- A01B 5/10 refers to ploughs equipped with rolling non-driven tools such as discs that are mounted or partly mounted on tractors. These implements belong to the soil-working group of agricultural machinery, allowing the tractor to pull the tool while the rolling elements cut or churn the soil.
[A01B 51/00] Parts, details or accessories of agricultural machines or implements, in general -- A01B 51/00 covers the general parts, details and accessories that are fitted to agricultural machines or implements, irrespective of the specific function of the component. It includes any auxiliary or replacement elements used on soil-working equipment in farming or forestry, without limiting the description to a particular type of machine or task.
[A01B 51/02] propelled by a motor -- This subclass includes parts, details or accessories of agricultural machines that are driven by a motor rather than by animal or human power, covering motor-propelled implements used for soil working in agriculture or forestry.
[A01B 51/023] of the Gantry-type -- A01B 51/023 refers to parts, details, or accessories of agricultural machines or implements that are specifically of the gantry type. This classification is part of a broader category dealing with soil working in agriculture and forestry under the Cooperative Patent Classification system used by the European Patent Office (EPO) and the United States Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO).
[A01B 51/026] of the automotive vehicle type, e.g. including driver accommodation  A01B51/023  takes precedence -- A01B 51/026 covers parts, details or accessories of agricultural machines or implements that are of an automotive-vehicle type, such as those incorporating driver accommodation. It is a sub-group of the broader category for general components of agricultural equipment, and its provisions are superseded wherever the more specific A01B 51/023 applies.
[A01B 51/04] drawn by animal or tractor -- A01B 51/04 designates parts, details or accessories of agricultural machines or implements used for soil working that are drawn by an animal or a tractor. It groups equipment intended for tillage, planting or cultivation where the pulling power comes from draft animals or motor-driven tractors.
[A01B 51/045] drawn by animals -- A01B 51/045 covers components or accessories for agricultural machines that are animal-drawn. It includes any parts specifically designed for implements pulled by draft animals such as horses, oxen, or mules, rather than motorized equipment.
[A01B 5/12] without alternating possibility -- A01B 5/12 classifies ploughs that incorporate rolling, non-driven implements such as discs and are designed without an alternating (reciprocating) function. It falls under Human Necessities → Agriculture → Soil Working in Agriculture or Forestry, specifically for ploughs using continuous rolling tools rather than alternating blades.
[A01B 5/14] Alternating ploughs -- Alternating ploughs are soil-working implements that carry rolling, non-driven tools such as discs arranged in alternating rows on opposite sides of the frame, allowing successive passes to cut and turn soil without powered rotation. They belong to the class of ploughs with rolling non-driven tools.

[A01B 59/00] Devices specially adapted for connection between animals or tractors and agricultural machines or implements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Specific details of tractor-implement hitches.  Details relating to electrical connectors for supplying electrical power to implements.
[A01B 59/002] Details, component parts -- A01B 59/002 classifies the individual details and component parts of coupling devices that connect animals or tractors to agricultural machines or implements, such as hitches, pins, clamps, and other attachment elements.
[A01B 59/004] Length-adjustable links -- Length-adjustable links are devices that connect animals or tractors to agricultural implements and can be altered in length to suit different pulling distances or terrain conditions, providing flexible coupling in soil-working operations.
[A01B 59/006] Latched hooks -- A01B 59/006 - Latched hooks refers to devices specially adapted to link animals or tractors with agricultural machines or implements using a latch mechanism that secures the connection and allows controlled release, facilitating reliable power transmission in soil-working operations.
[A01B 59/008] Link connectors with spherical surfaces -- A01B 59/008 covers link connectors that use spherical surfaces to join animals or tractors to agricultural implements, allowing pivoting and angular movement while transmitting power. These devices are designed for soil-working equipment and enable flexible, secure connections in farming and forestry operations.
[A01B 59/02] for animal-drawn machines -- A01B 59/02 covers devices designed to connect animal-drawn machines to agricultural tools or implements, such as hitches, coupling mechanisms and attachment fittings that enable efficient transmission of pulling force from livestock to soil-working equipment. This subcategory falls under soil-working devices specifically adapted for animal traction.
[A01B 59/04] for machines pulled or pushed by a tractor -- A01B 59/04 covers machines that are moved by a tractor, including implements that are pulled or pushed and the coupling devices that connect the tractor or draft animals to the implement.
[A01B 59/041] preventing or limiting side-play of implements -- A01B 59/041 covers mechanisms that reduce or prevent lateral movement of agricultural implements when they are attached to animals or tractors. It includes devices such as guides, braces or adjustable couplings designed to limit side-play, improving stability and control during operation.
[A01B 59/0415] by hydraulic or pneumatic means acting on the lower draft links -- A01B 59/0415 covers devices that use hydraulic or pneumatic power to act on the lower draft links linking animals or tractors to agricultural machines or implements, facilitating connection and control in soil-working operations. It falls under the subclass for coupling devices in agriculture.
[A01B 59/042] having pulling means arranged on the rear part of the tractor -- This subgroup of CPC covers tractors that incorporate pulling apparatus mounted at the rear, enabling the machine to tow agricultural tools or implements. It pertains to soil-working equipment where the pulling means are positioned behind the tractor chassis.
[A01B 59/043] supported at three points, e.g. by quick-release couplings -- A01B 59/043 covers devices that connect animals or tractors to agricultural machines or implements by supporting the implement at three points, typically using quick-release couplings. The classification falls under the human necessities hierarchy, agriculture, and soil-working sub-section, describing three-point linkage systems that facilitate rapid attachment and detachment of implements.
[A01B 59/044] having pulling means arranged on the middle part of the tractor -- A01B 59/044 designates tractors whose pulling means are placed on the middle part of the vehicle, providing a centrally located point for transmitting power to attached agricultural implements or animal-driven equipment. This arrangement improves balance and efficiency when connecting the tractor to various farming machines.
[A01B 59/046] having pulling or pushing means arranged on the side of the tractor -- A01B 59/046 covers devices that provide pulling or pushing mechanisms positioned on the side of a tractor, enabling side-mounted attachment of agricultural implements or animal-driven equipment. These side-mounted means facilitate lateral transfer of force between the tractor and the implement for tasks such as tillage, planting or harvesting.
[A01B 59/048] having pulling or pushing means arranged on the front part of the tractor -- A01B 59/048 defines tractors equipped with pulling or pushing devices located on the front of the machine, allowing the vehicle to link to and drive agricultural implements or animal-drawn equipment. It applies to front-mounted power transmission systems used in soil-working and related farming operations.
[A01B 59/06] for machines mounted on tractors -- The A01B 59/06 subclass covers devices that enable tractors or draught animals to connect with agricultural implements, specifically machines that are mounted directly on the tractor. It includes coupling mechanisms, hitch assemblies and related mounting hardware designed for attaching tools such as tillage, seeding or harvesting equipment to the tractor chassis. This subclass falls under soil-working devices in agriculture.
[A01B 59/061] specially adapted for enabling connection or disconnection controlled from the driver's seat  not used, see subgroups -- A01B 59/061 covers devices that allow the operator to connect or disconnect implements to a tractor or animal-driven machine from the driver's seat rather than manually at the hitch
[A01B 59/062] the connection comprising a rigid interface frame on the tractor -- A01B 59/062 covers a coupling system where the tractor carries a rigid interface frame that serves as the mounting point for agricultural implements. The frame provides a fixed, sturdy connection between the tractor and machines such as tillage tools, allowing precise alignment and secure power transfer during field operations. This type of device falls under soil-working equipment in the human necessities-agriculture hierarchy.
[A01B 59/063] the connection comprising longitudinally oriented tang-and-sheath elements -- It covers agricultural couplings that use a longitudinally oriented tang inserted into a matching sheath to connect tractors or draught animals with implements. The design provides a secure mechanical link that transmits torque and pulling forces while allowing quick attachment and detachment.
[A01B 59/064] for connection to the front of the tractor -- A01B 59/064 covers devices specially adapted to connect animals or tractors to agricultural machines or implements, specifically for mounting to the front of a tractor. These front-tractor coupling systems provide mechanical or hydraulic linkage for implements used in soil working.
[A01B 59/065] for ploughs or like implements  A01B59/061  takes precedence -- A01B 59/065 covers devices specially adapted to connect animals or tractors to ploughs or similar agricultural implements, fitting within the broader group of coupling devices for soil-working equipment. This subclass is subordinate to A01B 59/061, which takes precedence.
[A01B 59/066] of the type comprising at least two lower arms and one upper arm generally arranged in a triangle, e.g. three-point hitches -- A01B 59/066 defines three-point hitch devices that consist of at least two lower arms and one upper arm arranged in a triangular shape for connecting tractors or draft animals to agricultural implements. It is a subclass of soil-working tools within the human necessities and agriculture hierarchy.
[A01B 59/067] the lower arms being lifted or lowered by power actuator means internally incorporated in the tractor -- This CPC subclass covers tractors equipped with built-in power actuators that raise or lower the lower arms of attached implements. It belongs to the group of devices for connecting animals or tractors to agricultural machines within soil-working agriculture.
[A01B 59/068] the lower arms being lifted or lowered by power actuator means provided externally on the tractor -- The entry covers agricultural devices in which the lower arms of implements attached to a tractor are raised or lowered by power-actuator mechanisms that are mounted externally on the tractor, enabling controlled vertical movement of the implements during soil-working operations.
[A01B 59/069] with means for weighing the implement -- A01B 59/069 covers devices that connect animals or tractors to agricultural implements and incorporate means for weighing the implement during use. These systems provide real-time load measurement to improve control, efficiency and safety in soil-working operations.

It falls under the CPC hierarchy Human Necessities → Agriculture → Soil Working in Agriculture or Forestry.
[A01B 61/02] of the coupling devices between tractor and machine -- A01B 61/02 covers coupling devices that connect a tractor to an implement, including hitches, quick-connect systems and other mechanisms that transmit power while reducing strain on the tractor. The classification is placed in the agriculture part of the CPC under devices for preventing overstrain.

These devices ensure efficient torque transfer while helping to prevent overload and overstrain of the implement's moving elements.
[A01B 61/04] of the connection between tools and carrier beam or frame -- A01B 61/04 covers the means that join tools to a carrier beam or frame on agricultural or forestry machines, focusing on devices or parts designed to prevent over-strain during operation. [A01B 61/042] with shearing devices -- A01B 61/042 covers devices incorporated into soil-working agricultural or forestry machines that include shearing mechanisms to reduce operator strain, such as built-in cutters or slicers that cut plant material while the implement performs cultivation or tillage tasks.

The connection allows the implement to flex under load, reducing strain on the machine and improving operation on uneven terrain.
[A01B 61/046] the device including an energy accumulator for restoring the tool to its working position -- A01B 61/046 covers agricultural implements that incorporate an energy storage element such as a spring or hydraulic accumulator to automatically reposition a tool after it has been lifted or deflected, reducing operator effort and preventing overstrain. The device is used in soil-working machinery to restore the working tool to its operating position without manual intervention.
[A01B 61/048] the connection or the energy accumulator being active in two opposite directions, e.g. for reversible plows -- The subclass addresses mechanisms that let a power source or connection work in two opposite directions, allowing agricultural tools such as reversible plows to change the direction of motion without altering the energy accumulator. It covers designs where the same accumulator drives the implement forward and backward, enabling bidirectional operation in soil-working equipment.

[A01B 63/10] operated by hydraulic or pneumatic means -- A01B 63/10 includes hydraulic or pneumatic devices that lift or adjust agricultural machines and implements used for soil working, such as cylinders, rams or combined systems that raise, lower or tilt implements on tractors, sprayers or other equipment. [A01B 63/1013] and being located inside the tractor body -- A01B 63/1013 refers to lifting or adjusting devices that are mounted inside the tractor body, such as internal hydraulic or mechanical systems used to raise, lower or reposition agricultural implements while the tractor is in operation. [A01B 63/102] characterised by the location of the mounting on the tractor, e.g. on the rear part -- A01B 63/102 covers lifting or adjusting devices for agricultural machines whose mounting is defined by its position on the tractor, typically rear-mounted, and relates to soil-working equipment such as implements that are attached to the tractor's rear end.
[A01B 63/104] on the middle part, i.e. between front and rear wheels -- A01B 63/104 refers to lifting or adjusting devices that are mounted on the central portion of agricultural machines, situated between the front and rear wheels. [A01B 63/11] for controlling weight transfer between implements and tractor wheels -- A01B 63/11 covers mechanisms that manage the distribution of load between a tractor's wheels and attached implements, ensuring balanced weight transfer during operation. Such devices improve traction, stability and soil compaction control by adjusting how the implement's weight is shared with the tractor. This subclass falls under lifting or adjusting arrangements for agricultural machines within soil-working technologies.
[A01B 63/111] regulating working depth of implements -- A01B 63/111 covers devices and arrangements that regulate the working depth of soil-working implements, such as plows, seed drills or cultivators. These mechanisms-hydraulic, mechanical or sensor-based-adjust or maintain a preset penetration depth to ensure consistent performance and optimal soil interaction during agricultural operations.
[A01B 63/1112] using a non-tactile ground distance measurement, e.g. using reflection of waves -- A01B 63/1112 covers agricultural machines that use non-tactile ground-distance measurement, such as reflected acoustic or electromagnetic waves, to control lifting or positioning devices during soil-working operations. This enables automatic adjustment of implements without physical contact with the ground.

[A01B 63/112] to control draught load, i.e. tractive force -- A01B 63/112 covers devices that regulate the draught load or tractive force of agricultural machines, allowing the pulling resistance to be adjusted to suit soil conditions, implement demands or operator preferences.

[A01B 69/002] Steering or guiding on a substantially circular or spiral path -- A01B 69/002 covers methods and devices for steering agricultural or forestry machines so that they move along a substantially circular or spiral trajectory, such as for turning or spiraling maneuvers in fields. [A01B 69/003] Steering or guiding of machines or implements pushed or pulled by or mounted on agricultural vehicles such as tractors, e.g. by lateral shifting of the towing connection -- A01B 69/003 covers the steering or guiding of agricultural machines and implements that are pushed, pulled or mounted on tractors, for example by laterally shifting the towing connection to change direction.
[A01B 69/004] automatic -- A01B 69/004 is a CPC subclass for automatic steering of agricultural machines or implements.
