[A01G 23/00] Forestry -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Cultivation of fruit trees or the like -> A01G17/00
[A01G 23/003] Collecting felled trees -- A01G 23/003 covers techniques and apparatus for gathering trees that have been cut down, including systems for transporting, loading, and arranging felled timber in horticultural or forestry operations. It addresses the collection stage after felling and before further processing.
[A01G 23/006] Log skidders -- Log skidders are machines used in forestry to attach to felled trees and pull them from the forest floor to a collection point, often by cables, hooks or hydraulic grapples, enabling efficient timber extraction and transport. This equipment is classified under A01G 23/006, covering devices for moving logs and debris during cutting or gathering operations.
[A01G 23/02] Transplanting, uprooting, felling or delimbing trees -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Delimbing standing trees -> A01G3/00
[A01G 23/04] Transplanting trees -- A01G 23/04 refers to the techniques and apparatus used for moving trees, from digging up and lifting to transport and re-planting, including any preparatory measures that enhance the trees' survival after relocation.
[A01G 23/043] Transplanting devices for grasping, undercutting or transporting the root ball  A01G23/046  takes precedence -- A01G 23/043 covers devices used in horticulture for transplanting plants, specifically tools that grasp, undercut, or transport the root ball during removal and re-planting. These include spades, cages, and other apparatus that secure the soil-root mass to preserve plant health. The related subclass A01G 23/046, dealing with mechanisms for lifting or lowering the root ball, takes precedence over this entry.
[A01G 23/046] Transplanting devices using elements to be driven into the ground for making a container around the root ball -- A01G 23/046 covers transplanting devices that create a container around a plant's root ball by driving elements-such as spikes, rods or sleeves-into the soil, forming a temporary cage that secures the roots during movement. This technology is used in horticulture and forestry for safe plant relocation.
[A01G 23/06] Uprooting or pulling up trees -- The code A01G 23/06 covers methods and apparatus for removing trees from the ground, including techniques for uprooting or pulling them up. It relates to forestry operations where whole trees are extracted rather than trimmed or cut down, often for clearing land, transplanting, or harvesting root wood. This classification falls under horticulture within the broader human necessities category.
[A01G 23/062] Pulling up trees or stumps -- The A01G 23/062 classification covers methods and equipment for removing unwanted trees or stumps from the ground, including techniques for pulling them up and extracting root systems. It falls within the hierarchy of Human Necessities → Agriculture → Horticulture → Forestry, addressing the mechanical or manual processes used in forest management and land clearing.
[A01G 23/065] in a substantially vertical plane -- A01G 23/065 covers methods of planting or arranging forest, orchard or horticultural trees and shrubs so that they are placed mainly in a substantially vertical plane, such as on walls, fences or upright rows, rather than spread horizontally. It classifies techniques that create a primarily vertical planting configuration.
[A01G 23/067] by comminuting the tree stumps -- A01G 23/067 covers methods or apparatus that reduce tree stumps by comminuting them, such as chipping, grinding or crushing, to facilitate stump removal, land-clearing or biomass recovery. It falls within the forestry subclass of horticultural techniques aimed at managing harvested trees and preparing sites for further agricultural use.
[A01G 23/08] Felling trees -- A01G 23/08 pertains to techniques and equipment for felling trees, encompassing methods that enable safe and efficient cutting, harvesting, and handling of timber in forestry operations. The classification includes specialized tools, machines, and processes designed to sever trees and prepare the wood for transport or further processing.
[A01G 23/081] Feller-bunchers, i.e. with bunching by felling head -- A01G 23/081 refers to feller-buncher machines used in forestry that combine a cutting head with a bunching mechanism, allowing a tree to be felled and its trunk automatically gathered into a bunch for easier transport or processing.
[A01G 23/083] Feller-delimbers -- Feller-delimbers are machines or tools that cut standing trees or shrubs and remove their branches, producing a clean trunk ready for transport or further processing. They are employed in forestry and horticultural work to fell and debranch vegetation efficiently.
[A01G 23/085] having the shearing head mounted on a first boom and the delimbing head mounted on a second boom -- The subclass A01G 23/085 covers combined harvesting machines in which the shearing head is mounted on a first boom while a separate delimbing head is attached to a second boom, allowing simultaneous cutting and debarking of trees or shrubs. This arrangement is used in horticultural and forestry operations to improve efficiency by coordinating two functional units on a single mobile platform.
[A01G 23/087] Shearing apparatus or the like specially adapted for felling trees -- A01G 23/087 covers devices designed to cut or shear trees, such as specialized shears, saws, or cutting heads that are mounted on handles, machines or other apparatuses to facilitate felling. The classification includes adaptations that improve safety, efficiency or ease of use when removing trees in forestry or horticultural operations.
[A01G 23/089] having two or more shears -- A01G 23/089 covers agricultural implements that employ two or more shears to cut or weed vegetation, such as rotary cultivators or multi-blade weeders. The code applies to mechanical devices where multiple cutting elements operate together to remove unwanted plant material in horticulture and forestry.
[A01G 23/09] of the percussion type -- A01G 23/09 covers horticultural or forestry implements that plant, transplant or sow by means of a percussive action. The devices use a hammer-like strike to drive seeds, seedlings or cuttings into the soil, such as percussion seed drills or transplanting tools that rely on impact rather than pressure. This subclass falls under the broader categories of human necessities, agriculture, horticulture and forestry.
[A01G 23/091] Sawing apparatus specially adapted for felling trees -- A01G 23/091 covers saws and cutting devices designed specifically for felling trees, including portable chainsaws, mechanical saws and related handling equipment used in forestry operations. The classification addresses adaptations that improve safety, efficiency or suitability for cutting standing timber.
[A01G 23/093] Combinations of shearing, sawing or milling apparatus specially adapted for felling trees -- A01G 23/093 covers forestry equipment that integrates shearing, sawing or milling mechanisms into a single system specifically designed for cutting down trees. The classification includes combined cutting heads, blade‐saw assemblies, or multi-function tools that can shear and saw or mill tree trunks in one operation. It relates to machines that improve efficiency and safety in tree-felling tasks.
[A01G 23/095] Delimbers -- Delimbers are tools or devices used to remove side shoots, suckers or branches from trees, shrubs, and other woody plants to shape growth and improve yield. In horticulture and forestry they are employed for pruning, training, and maintaining plant form, often featuring blades or hooks that cut or pull off unwanted growth.
[A01G 23/0955] Self-propelled along standing trees -- The CPC subclass A01G 23/0955, under Human Necessities → Agriculture → Horticulture → Forestry, covers machines or devices that move themselves along standing trees, such as self-propelled sprayers, harvesters or treatment units that travel on the trunks or branches while performing operations like spraying, pruning or harvesting. This subclass defines the technical features of the propulsion and the ability to operate on trees that remain in place.
[A01G 23/097] having a fixed delimbing head -- A01G 23/097 covers agricultural machines, particularly orchard or forestry equipment, that incorporate a delimbing device whose cutting head is permanently attached to the machine and does not swivel or detach during operation. The fixed delimbing head is designed to strip branches or foliage from trees as the vehicle moves through the stand, improving efficiency in harvesting or pruning.
[A01G 23/099] Auxiliary devices, e.g. felling wedges -- A01G 23/099 covers auxiliary devices used in tree cutting and removal, such as felling wedges and similar implements that assist in splitting or directing a standing tree during the felling process. These tools are classified under horticulture and forestry applications for managing tree harvesting.
[A01G 23/10] Tapping of tree-juices, e.g. caoutchouc, gum -- This CPC subclass covers methods and apparatus for extracting liquids from trees, such as natural rubber (caoutchouc) or gum, by tapping. It includes techniques for incising bark, collecting sap, and related equipment.
[A01G 23/12] Knives or axes for tapping -- A01G 23/12 covers knives, axes or similar cutting tools specifically designed for tapping, the process of making incisions in trees or woody plants to collect sap or stimulate growth. These implements are used in horticulture and forestry for tasks such as maple-sap extraction or bark removal, and are distinguished from general-purpose cutting tools by features that facilitate precise, controlled tapping.
[A01G 23/14] Tapping-spouts -- A01G 23/14 covers devices that draw fluids from plants, such as taps, tubes, or spouts used to collect sap, latex, or other exudates from trees and shrubs. The classification includes designs that attach to the plant, regulate flow, and may incorporate sealing or collection containers, facilitating commercial or experimental harvesting of plant-derived liquids.
[A01G 2/32] Automatic apparatus therefor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus for mechanically grafting plants, operating partly or totally automatically.  Devices for mechanically cutting, inserting or ligating the scion and/or the stock, operating partly or totally automatically (illustration in Fig.1).  Seedling grafting robots (illustration in Fig.2).
[A01G 2/35] Cutting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus for manually (illustration in Fig.1) or automatically (illustration in Fig.2) cutting the scion and/or stock.  Apparatus for manually or automatically inserting the scion into the stock for joining them into a graft joint.  Methods therefor.
[A01G 2/38] Holding -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus for manually or automatically holding the graft joint in position through ligating or clamping means, e.g. with clamps, clips, etc. (illustrations in Fig.1 and Fig.2);  Methods therefor.
[A01G 24/00] Growth substrates -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Culture media for mushrooms -> A01G18/20
[A01G 24/10] based on or containing inorganic material -- A01G 24/10 pertains to growth substrates used in horticulture that are based on or contain inorganic materials, such as minerals or synthetic compounds, which provide structural support and nutrient delivery for plant roots.
[A01G 24/12] containing soil minerals -- A01G 24/12 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to growth substrates that contain soil minerals, used in horticulture for enhancing plant growth and root development.
[A01G 24/13] Zeolites -- A01G 24/13 covers the use of zeolite materials as growth substrates in horticulture. Zeolites are porous aluminosilicates that improve moisture retention, nutrient exchange and aeration in plant media, supporting soil-less cultivation.
[A01G 24/15] Calcined rock, e.g. perlite, vermiculite or clay aggregates -- A01G 24/15 classifies the use of calcined rock materials-including perlite, vermiculite and clay aggregates-as growth substrates in horticultural production. These inert, lightweight aggregates improve aeration, drainage and water retention of planting media, supporting plant development in soil-free or container systems.
[A01G 24/17] containing slag -- A01G 24/17 covers horticultural growth substrates that incorporate slag, the waste material produced in metal smelting. It includes plant media where slag is mixed with other components to improve physical or chemical properties for soil-free cultivation, such as drainage, aeration or nutrient supply.
[A01G 24/18] containing inorganic fibres, e.g. mineral wool -- A01G 24/18 covers horticultural growth substrates that incorporate inorganic fibers such as mineral wool. These substrates are designed to provide physical support, aeration and water-retention for plant roots while improving durability and resistance to decay. They are used in greenhouse media, hydroponics and other controlled-environment cultivation systems.
[A01G 24/20] based on or containing natural organic material -- A01G 24/20 covers horticultural growth substrates that are formulated with or primarily consist of natural organic material such as peat, compost, or other biodegradable plant residues, used to support the cultivation of crops, trees, or ornamental plants.
[A01G 24/22] containing plant material -- A01G 24/22 covers growth substrates that incorporate plant material, such as compost, peat, straw or other organic residues, used to support the cultivation of crops and ornamental plants. The classification applies to media where the plant-derived component is an integral part of the substrate's composition, providing nutrients, structure or moisture-retention properties for horticultural production.
[A01G 24/23] Wood, e.g. wood chips or sawdust -- This subclass deals with using wood-derived materials such as chips, shavings or sawdust as growth substrates in horticulture. It covers methods and compositions where wood particles serve as a medium for planting, rooting or providing nutrient support to crops and ornamental plants.
[A01G 24/25] Dry fruit hulls or husks, e.g. chaff or coir -- This subclass concerns the use of dried fruit hulls or husks such as chaff, coir and similar organic materials as growth substrates in horticulture. These dry, fibrous residues serve as low-cost, aerating media that retain moisture and support plant rooting in agricultural production.
[A01G 24/27] Pulp, e.g. bagasse -- A01G 24/27 covers the use of pulp materials, such as bagasse derived from sugar-cane processing, as a growth substrate for horticultural plants. It refers to formulations where the pulp serves as the main medium for rooting, planting or transplanting, often mixed with other components to improve aeration and water retention.
[A01G 24/28] containing peat, moss or sphagnum -- A01G 24/28 covers plant growth substrates that incorporate peat, moss or sphagnum as the primary medium. It includes formulations, preparation methods and uses of these organic materials to support horticultural cultivation.
[A01G 24/30] based on or containing synthetic organic compounds -- A01G 24/30 pertains to horticultural growth substrates that incorporate synthetic organic compounds, such as polymers, surfactants or nutrient carriers, to enhance properties like water retention, aeration or nutrient delivery for cultivated plants.
[A01G 24/35] containing water-absorbing polymers -- This CPC subgroup covers horticultural growth substrates that incorporate water-absorbing polymers to retain moisture and improve water availability for plants. It includes formulations where polymers are mixed into soil, peat, rockwool, or other media to enhance water-holding capacity and reduce irrigation frequency.
[A01G 24/40] characterised by their structure -- A01G 24/40 covers growth substrates for horticulture that are distinguished primarily by their structural characteristics such as shape, porosity, particle size or composition, rather than by chemical additives or biological components. This includes substrates engineered to provide specific physical support, aeration or water-holding properties for plants.
[A01G 24/42] of granular or aggregated structure -- A01G 24/42 covers growth substrates that have a granular or aggregated structure, such as peat, compost, perlite, vermiculite, rockwool or similar materials formed into particles or clumps for use in horticultural cultivation. It classifies methods and compositions where the substrate's physical form is composed of distinct granules or aggregates rather than a solid mass.
[A01G 24/44] in block, mat or sheet form -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Compressed growth substrates, e.g. compressed pots, blocks, slabs, bricks, plugs, pellets, wafers, mats, boards (illustrations in Fig.1).  Growth substrates with a coherent shape, e.g. cured with a binder.  Fig.1: Compressed growth substrates
[A01G 24/46] multi-layered -- A01G 24/46 belongs to the hierarchy Human necessities ► Agriculture ► Horticulture ► Growth substrates and defines multi-layered substrates made of two or more distinct layers that provide differing physical or chemical conditions for plant growth, such as separate zones for water retention, nutrient supply, aeration and mechanical support, commonly used in hydroponic and other soil-less cultivation systems.
[A01G 24/48] containing foam or presenting a foam structure -- A01G 24/48 covers horticultural growing substrates that incorporate foam or have a foam-like structure, such as foam-filled containers, foamed peat blocks, polymeric foam mats or other aerated foam media used to support plant roots. The classification applies to any substrate where the foam component is integral to its function.
[A01G 24/50] contained within a flexible envelope -- A01G 24/50 covers horticultural growth substrates that are supplied in a flexible envelope such as a bag, pouch or film. The classification includes pre-packaged mixes of peat, coir, mineral or other media that can be easily handled, transported and opened for planting.
[A01G 24/60] Apparatus for preparing growth substrates or culture media -- A01G 24/60 refers to devices used in horticulture to prepare growth substrates or culture media, encompassing equipment that mixes, conditions, sterilises or otherwise treats the material before it is applied to plants. The classification covers both laboratory-scale and commercial-scale apparatus designed specifically for creating the nutrient or physical base required for plant cultivation.
[A01G 25/00] Watering gardens, fields, sports grounds or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a concealed elevated irrigation system. [A01G 25/003] with an impermeable layer in the ground -- A01G 25/003 covers irrigation techniques that employ an impermeable layer-such as plastic film, clay or other waterproof material-placed in or beneath the soil to limit water loss, improve distribution and raise water-use efficiency, commonly used for garden beds, potted plants and other horticultural applications.
[A01G 25/006] Tubular drip irrigation dispensers mounted coaxially within water feeding tubes -- A tubular drip-irrigation dispenser mounted coaxially inside a water-feeding tube provides a compact, protected means of delivering precise water droplets directly to plants. The design integrates the emitters within the supply line, reducing exposure to damage and improving uniform distribution across gardens, fields or sports grounds.
[A01G 25/02] Watering arrangements located above the soil which make use of perforated pipe-lines or pipe-lines with dispensing fittings, e.g. for drip irrigation -- A01G 25/02 classifies irrigation setups that supply water from above the soil through perforated pipes or pipelines equipped with dispensing fittings, such as drip-irrigation systems that release water via small openings directly onto the surface.
[A01G 25/023] Dispensing fittings for drip irrigation, e.g. drippers -- The code covers devices that regulate and deliver water in drip-irrigation systems, such as drippers and other fittings that control flow to plants in gardens, fields or sports grounds. These components are designed to dispense precise amounts of water at low pressure, improving water-use efficiency in horticultural and agricultural applications.
[A01G 25/026] Apparatus or processes for fitting the drippers to the hoses or the pipes -- A01G 25/026 covers devices and methods for attaching drip-irrigation emitters to hoses or pipes, enabling precise water delivery in gardens, fields or sports grounds. It belongs to the horticulture subclass dealing with watering systems.
[A01G 25/06] Watering arrangements making use of perforated pipe-lines located in the soil -- The code A01G 25/06 pertains to watering systems that employ perforated pipelines buried in the soil to distribute water to gardens, fields or sports grounds. It is classified under horticulture in the agriculture sector and addresses subsurface irrigation arrangements.
[A01G 25/09] Watering arrangements making use of movable installations on wheels or the like -- A01G 25/09 covers watering systems that incorporate movable devices such as wheeled or tracked installations to deliver water to gardens, fields, sports grounds or similar areas. These arrangements are designed to be repositioned easily, allowing flexible irrigation across varied terrain.
[A01G 25/092] movable around a pivot centre -- The code A01G 25/092 refers to irrigation equipment that can be moved while rotating around a fixed pivot point, allowing water to be directed over gardens, fields or sports grounds. Such devices are used in horticultural practices to adjust the spray pattern without relocating the whole system.
[A01G 25/095] winch-driven  A01G25/097  takes precedence -- A01G 25/095 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to winch-driven systems for watering gardens, fields, sports grounds or similar areas, with a note that A01G 25/097 takes precedence when applicable.
[A01G 25/097] guided or propelled along a water supply line with supply line traversing means -- A01G 25/097 covers devices that are guided or propelled along a water supply line while the line itself is traversed across the area to be irrigated, such as rollers, sleds or carts that move on the pipe or conduit to distribute water over gardens, fields or sports grounds.
[A01G 25/14] Hand watering devices, e.g. watering cans -- A01G 25/14 covers hand-operated watering devices such as watering cans that are used to irrigate gardens, fields, sports grounds or similar areas. These portable, manual tools deliver water directly to plants for small-scale or spot watering.
[A01G 25/145] with pumps -- A01G 25/145 covers devices and methods for irrigating gardens, fields, sports grounds or similar areas by means of pumps. It belongs to the horticulture subgroup of agriculture within the human necessities category, focusing on pump-driven watering systems.
[A01G 25/16] Control of watering -- The A01G 25/16 class covers systems and methods for regulating the application of water to plants, including devices that control timing, flow rate, pressure or distribution patterns in irrigation of gardens, fields, sports grounds or similar areas. It encompasses automated controllers, sensors, valves and other mechanisms that adjust watering to meet specific conditions.
[A01G 25/162] Sequential operation -- Sequential operation refers to irrigation systems that water distinct zones or sections of a garden, field or sports ground one after another rather than simultaneously. This approach conserves water and energy by delivering a timed, moving stream of water across the area, allowing precise control of flow and coverage.
[A01G 25/165] Cyclic operations, timing systems, timing valves, impulse operations -- A01G 25/165 pertains to irrigation systems that operate in cycles, including timing mechanisms, timing valves and impulse-type controls used for watering gardens, fields or sports grounds. The code covers devices that regulate start-stop intervals, dose delivery and automated sequencing of water application.

[A01G 3/00] Cutting implements specially adapted for horticultural purposes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Tools and machines for cutting plants with the purpose of pruning, trimming, shaping, etc.  Also shredders for plant waste.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Forestry delimbers -> A01G23/095
[A01G 3/002] for comminuting plant waste -- This CPC subclass covers cutting implements specially adapted for horticultural use that comminute plant waste such as pruned branches, stalks and other residues, facilitating their disposal, recycling or composting.
[A01G 3/02] Secateurs -- Secateurs are hand-held cutting tools designed for horticultural pruning, featuring a lever-action mechanism that brings two sharp blades together to shear stems, shoots or small branches. They are used in gardening, fruit orchards and vineyards to trim plants without damaging surrounding tissue.
[A01G 3/021] characterized by the arrangement of pivots -- A01G 3/021 covers cutting tools specially adapted for horticultural use that are defined by their pivot arrangement, such as pruning shears, loppers or similar implements where the blade and handle are linked by one or more pivots to provide improved leverage and precision.
[A01G 3/025] having elongated or extended handles -- A01G 3/025 designates horticultural cutting tools that are equipped with elongated or extended handles, providing greater reach and leverage for pruning or harvesting tasks. It is a subclass of cutting implements specially adapted for horticultural purposes within the agriculture category.
[A01G 3/0251] Loppers, i.e. branch cutters with two handles  hedge shears  A01G3/0475 -- A01G 3/0251 in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers loppers and hedge shears, which are cutting tools specifically designed for horticultural use, featuring two handles for efficient branch trimming and shaping of hedges.
[A01G 3/0255] Tree pruners, i.e. pruning shears carried at the end of a pole -- A01G 3/0255 covers pole-mounted tree pruners, which are pruning shears attached to the end of a pole for reaching high branches. These tools are designed specifically for horticultural pruning tasks, allowing users to cut branches without climbing a ladder. The classification falls under cutting implements for horticultural purposes within the agriculture section of the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[A01G 3/033] having motor-driven blades -- A01G 3/033 covers horticultural cutting tools that incorporate motor-driven blades, such as powered hedge trimmers, electric pruners and mechanised shears, designed specifically for use in agricultural gardening and plant cultivation. The classification groups these powered implements within the broader horticulture and agriculture sections.
[A01G 3/0335] having elongated or extended handles -- A01G 3/0335 falls under the CPC group for horticultural cutting tools and specifies implements that feature elongated or extended handles, facilitating reach and leverage when pruning or harvesting plants. This subclass refines the broader category of cutting implements specially adapted for horticultural purposes.
[A01G 3/037] the driving means being an electric motor -- A01G 3/037 covers horticultural cutting tools that are powered by an electric motor, such as electric pruners, hedge shears, or other electric-driven implements designed specifically for garden and orchard work. The classification pertains to cutting implements specially adapted for horticultural purposes where the drive mechanism is electric rather than manual, pneumatic or hydraulic.
[A01G 3/04] Apparatus for trimming hedges, e.g. hedge shears -- A01G 3/04 covers cutting tools designed specifically for horticultural use, such as hedge shears and other apparatus for trimming hedges. [A01G 3/0417] Guiding frames for trimming hedges -- Guiding frames for trimming hedges are structures attached to cutting implements used in horticulture to maintain shape and direction while pruning. They consist of adjustable arms or rails that hold the tool at a set angle, ensuring consistent cuts along hedge lines. This facilitates efficient, uniform hedge trimming.
[A01G 3/0426] Machines for pruning vegetation on embankments and road-sides -- A01G 3/0426 covers machines designed to prune or trim vegetation growing on embankments, slopes or road-sides. These specialized cutting devices are used in horticulture to keep elevated or linear areas clear of overgrowth and maintain safe, accessible pathways.
[A01G 3/0435] Machines specially adapted for shaping plants, e.g. topiaries -- The A01G 3/0435 entry covers machines designed to shape and sculpt living plants, such as topiary, by using specialized cutting tools, automated arms or other mechanisms that trim and contour foliage with precision for ornamental horticultural purposes.
[A01G 3/047] portable -- This subclass covers portable tools such as hand pruners, secateurs, shears and similar handheld cutting devices designed for use in horticulture, including gardens, orchards and nurseries. They are intended to be carried and operated manually rather than mounted.
[A01G 3/0475] Hedge shears -- Hedge shears are handheld cutting tools designed for trimming and shaping hedgerows and shrubs in horticulture. They feature two opposing blades pivoted together, allowing precise, clean cuts for plant maintenance.
[A01G 3/053] motor-driven -- A01G 3/053 covers motor-driven cutting implements specially adapted for horticultural purposes, such as powered pruners, shears or mowers used to cut stems, branches or other plant material in gardens, orchards and vineyards. These tools are powered by electric or internal-combustion engines.
[A01G 3/0535] with rotatable knives -- The CPC subclass A01G 3/0535 covers cutting implements designed for horticultural use that incorporate rotatable knives, allowing continuous or intermittent blade movement to improve cutting efficiency. Such tools are typically employed for pruning, trimming or harvesting of plants where a rotating cutting action offers advantages over fixed blades.

[A01G 3/067] Motor-driven shears for lawns -- Motor-driven lawn shears are powered cutting tools designed to trim and maintain grass surfaces, using a motor-generated shearing action to cut foliage cleanly and efficiently. They are adapted for horticultural use, allowing rapid, uniform mowing of lawns and similar groundcovers, and typically feature rotating blades or reciprocating cutters driven by electric or internal-combustion engines.
[A01G 3/08] Other tools for pruning, branching or delimbing standing trees -- A01G 3/08 relates to tools used to prune, branch or delimb standing trees, encompassing hand-held, pole-mounted or other specialized devices that cut or shape tree limbs while the tree remains rooted.
[A01G 3/081] Manual cutting tools not otherwise provided for, e.g. pruning hooks -- A01G 3/081 covers manually operated cutting tools designed specifically for horticultural tasks that are not classified elsewhere, such as pruning hooks and other specialized hand-held implements used to trim or shape plants.

[A01G 3/085] Motor-driven saws for pruning or branching -- A01G 3/085 covers motor-driven saws designed for pruning or branching in horticultural work. These powered cutting tools are used to trim plants, shrubs or trees, providing efficient removal of branches.
[A01G 3/086] Chain saws -- This subclass includes chain-saw devices designed as cutting implements for horticultural use, such as pruning, felling or trimming trees, vines and other plants. It covers portable saws with a moving chain that cuts wood or similar material, intended specifically for agricultural and gardening applications.
[A01G 3/088] Circular saws -- A01G 3/088 refers to circular saws designed for horticultural use, such as cutting, pruning or harvesting woody plants, vines and other vegetation. The class includes motor-driven or hand-operated circular blades adapted to agricultural or garden settings, and excludes general-purpose woodworking saws. It falls under cutting implements specially adapted for horticultural purposes.
[A01G 31/00] Soilless cultivation, e.g. hydroponics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cultivation of plants in absence of soil. [A01G 31/008] Control or regulation thereof -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Soilless cultivation having systems to control or alter nutrient properties, including, but not limited to, flow rate, volume of fluid, liquid level or control of valves. It includes manual or automated transplanters, seed-ling trays, planting cups and other devices that lift and position plants for potting in greenhouse or indoor horticultural settings.
[A01G 9/085] Devices for setting seeds in pots -- A01G 9/085 covers tools and apparatus used to place seeds into containers such as pots, trays, or other receptacles for horticultural cultivation. It includes seed-sowing devices that automate or assist the precise deposition of seeds in greenhouse or indoor growing systems, improving planting efficiency and uniformity.
[A01G 9/086] Devices for repotting -- A01G 9/086 pertains to horticultural equipment designed for repotting, that is, moving plants from one container into another during cultivation in pots, frames or greenhouses. It includes tools, mechanisms and automated devices that assist in transferring seedlings, cutting back roots and placing plants in new receptacles.
[A01G 9/088] Handling or transferring pots -- A01G 9/088 covers methods and devices for moving, placing, or otherwise handling horticultural containers such as pots, trays, and planters during cultivation, especially in greenhouse or forced-frame environments. It includes tools, mechanisms, or systems used to lift, transport, or reposition pots to facilitate planting, maintenance, or harvesting operations.
[A01G 9/12] Supports for plants -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Stays for trees, props for vines -> A01G17/14
[A01G 9/122] Stakes -- Stakes are supporting elements inserted into the growing medium of potted, frame-grown or greenhouse-cultivated plants to anchor stems, vines or branches and maintain proper orientation and spacing. They may be simple rods, pins, spikes or more complex devices that can be driven or placed in the substrate, facilitating plant stability, training and ease of handling during cultivation. This class covers all such supportive implements used in horticultural receptacle or protected-environment production.
[A01G 9/124] Means for holding stakes upright in, on, or beside pots -- A01G 9/124 covers devices designed to keep supporting stakes vertical while they are placed in, on, or beside plant containers such as pots, receptacles, forcing-frames or greenhouse trays. The means may include brackets, clips, sleeves or other fixtures that secure the stake to the container or surrounding structure, facilitating stable plant support during growth.
[A01G 9/126] Wirespool supports -- A01G 9/126 relates to horticultural systems where wire spools are employed as plant supports within containers, frames or greenhouse environments. The code covers designs and methods using rolled-wire structures to hold or train stems, vines or trellised crops during cultivation.

It covers the design, arrangement and operation of these fixation mechanisms that hold stems, vines or branches in place during cultivation, especially in containers, forcing-frames or greenhouses.
[A01G 9/14] Greenhouses -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Enclosed structures providing a regulated, e.g. warmer or plant-growing environment. The structures are constructed with transparent or translucent roofs and walls to allow solar radiation to pass through and warm the interior and enclosed to prevent warmed air from escaping. [A01G 9/1438] Covering materials therefor; Materials for protective coverings used for soil and plants, e.g. films, canopies, tunnels or cloches -- This CPC subclass concerns protective coverings such as films, canopies, tunnels or cloches used in horticultural cultivation in containers, forcing frames or greenhouses. It covers materials that shield soil and plants from weather, pests, light or other environmental factors.
[A01G 9/1469] with double or multiple walls -- A01G 9/1469 covers horticultural structures such as containers, forcing frames or greenhouses that use double or multiple walls for improved insulation, temperature control or protection. This falls under the broader CPC group for cultivation in receptacles, forcing-frames or greenhouses.
[A01G 9/1476] Greenhouse gutters  gutters in general  E04D13/064 -- A01G 9/1476 pertains to greenhouse gutters, encompassing the design and installation of drainage systems for managing water runoff from greenhouse roofs, which is crucial for preventing water damage and efficiently collecting rainwater. This classification also covers general aspects of gutters as found in building construction under E04D13/064.
[A01G 9/16] Dismountable or portable greenhouses -- A01G 9/16 pertains to greenhouse structures that are designed to be taken apart or moved, including portable and demountable frames used for cultivating plants in controlled environments. These systems enable quick assembly, relocation, and storage, supporting seasonal or temporary horticultural production.
[A01G 9/18] Greenhouses for treating plants with carbon dioxide or the like -- A01G 9/18 relates to greenhouse structures equipped to enrich the internal atmosphere with carbon dioxide or similar gases in order to accelerate plant growth, improve yield, or enhance quality. The classification covers systems for generating, injecting, and controlling such gases alongside other climate-control features within the greenhouse.
[A01G 9/20] Forcing-frames -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Low gardening beds, with covering frames like glass panes, for forcing or propagating young plants (illustration in Fig.1).  Covering frames for use in forcing-frames, e.g. glass frames, etc. (illustration in Fig.2). [A01G 9/22] Shades or blinds for greenhouses, or the like -- Shades, blinds or similar light-filtering devices used in greenhouses or other cultivation structures to regulate solar radiation, temperature and ventilation. The entry covers fixed or movable panels, screens, curtains or nettings designed to protect plants from excessive light or heat while allowing controlled illumination.
[A01G 9/222] Lamellar or like blinds  in general  E06B9/26 -- A01G 9/222 pertains to lamellar or similar blinds used in horticultural structures such as greenhouses, forcing frames or containers. [A01G 9/225] Inflatable structures -- A01G 9/225 covers the use of inflatable structures such as air-filled covers, domes or balloons to create a controlled growing environment for plants cultivated in receptacles, forcing frames or greenhouses. These structures provide temporary, flexible protection and climate regulation, enabling year-round or seasonal horticultural production in enclosed or semi-enclosed settings.
[A01G 9/227] rolled up during non-use  in general  E06B9/08 -- A01G 9/227 relates to the practice of rolling up greenhouse or forcing-frame structures when they are not in use, describing mechanisms or procedures that allow the covering or frame to be compacted for storage, transport or protection during periods of inactivity. This subclass falls under horticultural cultivation in receptacles and greenhouses, linking to the broader concept of temporary closure of such structures.
[A01G 9/24] Devices for heating, ventilating, regulating temperature , or watering, in greenhouses, forcing-frames, or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Systems or devices for managing the environment in greenhouses, forcing-frames or the like, such as:  Ventilating systems for greenhouses or the like.  Heating systems.  Air-conditioning systems.  Illumination arrangements other than for a lighting treatment of the plant.  Watering arrangements of plants cultivated in greenhouses or the like.
[A01G 9/241] Arrangement of opening or closing systems for windows and ventilation panels -- This subclass covers the design and placement of mechanisms that open or close windows, vents or panels in greenhouse and other controlled-environment horticultural installations. It includes arrangements of hinges, sliding tracks, automatic regulators and seals that manage airflow and climate for plants grown in containers, forcing frames or greenhouses.
[A01G 9/242] for greenhouses with flexible coverings -- A01G 9/242 pertains to greenhouse constructions that employ flexible covering materials-such as plastic film, shade cloth or inflatable membranes-to protect and control the climate for plants cultivated in containers, frames or other forced-growth environments.
[A01G 9/243] Collecting solar energy  in general  F24S -- A01G 9/243 covers methods for gathering solar energy to supply heat or power to horticultural systems such as containers, forcing frames, or greenhouses. It includes solar collectors, thermal storage, and distribution arrangements used to improve plant-growth conditions.
[A01G 9/245] Conduits for heating by means of liquids, e.g. used as frame members or for soil heating -- A01G 9/245 covers conduits that carry heating liquids and are incorporated into frames or other structures for warming soil or plants, typically used in greenhouse, forcing-frame or container cultivation. The classification applies to devices where the liquid-filled pipes themselves form part of the growing environment's heating system.
[A01G 9/246] Air-conditioning systems -- Air-conditioning systems refer to equipment and methods for controlling temperature, humidity, ventilation and gas composition within greenhouse or other enclosed cultivation environments. The entry includes heating, cooling, dehumidifying, evaporative-cooling, ventilation and related control devices used to create optimal growing conditions for plants grown in receptacles, forcing frames or glasshouses.
[A01G 9/247] Watering arrangements -- A01G 9/247 relates to watering arrangements used in horticultural cultivation within receptacles, forcing-frames or greenhouses. It covers systems and devices that deliver water to plants grown in these controlled environments, including irrigation methods, distribution networks and control mechanisms.
[A01G 9/249] Lighting means  with special arrangements for promoting growth  A01G7/045 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Spatial arrangements of lamps for controlling the light propagation direction towards the plants, e.g. to increase photosynthetic activity, such as:  Vertical lamp arrays (illustration in Fig.1),  Lighting means for illuminating the bottom leaves,  Movable illumination means, etc.  Illumination systems with structural features for controlling the light propagation direction towards the plants, such as:  Screens, shades in association with horticultural lamps;  Horticultural lamps with lenses;  Horticultural lamps with reflectors (illustration in Fig.2), etc.  Horticultural illumination systems with cooling means (illustration in Fig.3).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Lighting treatment of plants -> A01G7/045
[A01G 9/26] Electric devices -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Lighting means -> A01G9/249
[A01G 9/28] Raised beds -- A01G 9/28 covers the design and use of raised beds for horticultural cultivation, including soils that are elevated above the surrounding ground level, often bounded by borders or structures. It encompasses methods for constructing, filling and managing these beds to improve drainage, soil quality and plant access, typically applied in gardens, farms and greenhouse settings.
[A01H] NEW PLANTS OR PROCESSES FOR OBTAINING THEM -- **Definition:** This place covers:  New non-transgenic plants (including multicellular algae, multicellular fungi and lichens), plant varieties, plant tissue culture and breeding methods, methods for altering phenotypes.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Unicellular algae -> C12N1/12 - Unicellular, single celled fungi -> C12N1/14 - Specific mutations prepared by genetic engineering on plant cell or plant tissues -> C12N15/00
[A01H 1/00] Processes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Breeding methods as further defined by the sub-groups.  Plants characterized by non-agronomic quality traits, agronomic yield or resistance aspects.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Plant reproduction by tissue culture techniques -> A01H4/00
[A01H 1/02] Methods or apparatus for hybridisation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Creation of hybrids, interspecific crossing, genic (e.g. male or female A01H1/022 – A01H1/024 ) and nongenic (e.g. by using chemicals A01H1/026 ) sterility systems. Apparatus for pollination in A01H1/027 .
[A01H 1/021] Methods of breeding using interspecific crosses, i.e. interspecies crosses -- A01H 1/021 covers techniques for breeding new plant varieties through interspecific, or interspecies, crosses. It includes methods that facilitate the combination of genetic material from different plant species to create hybrids with desired traits, encompassing the steps required to achieve successful cross-pollination, seed development, and selection of offspring.
[A01H 1/022] Genic fertility modification, e.g. apomixis -- A01H 1/022 covers methods for genetically altering plant fertility, notably inducing apomixis or other forms of reproductive bypass so that seeds develop without conventional fertilization. It includes techniques that modify genes governing gamete formation, embryogenesis or seed development to enable clonal propagation through seed. This facilitates rapid multiplication of elite plant varieties while preserving genetic identity.
[A01H 1/023] Male sterility -- A01H 1/023 covers processes that create or utilize male-sterile plants, enabling controlled pollination and hybrid seed production. It includes techniques for inducing sterility, managing sterile lines, and exploiting male sterility in breeding programs to improve crop yields and genetic diversity.
[A01H 1/024] Female sterility -- A01H 1/024 covers inventions concerned with inducing or managing sterility in female plants, including methods, treatments, or genetic modifications that prevent female reproductive development. It is used to control breeding, simplify seed production, or enhance crop yields, and falls within the CPC group for new plants and processes for obtaining them.
[A01H 1/026] by treatment with chemicals -- A01H 1/026 covers processes for obtaining new plants that involve treating plant material with chemicals, such as applying growth regulators, hormones, or other chemical agents to seeds, cuttings, or tissue cultures to induce desired traits or improve development. This subclass falls under the broader category of agricultural methods for producing new plant varieties.
[A01H 1/027] Apparatus for pollination -- The CPC entry A01H 1/027 covers devices designed to facilitate pollination of crops, such as mechanical vibrators, brushes, pneumatic or fluid-based applicators, and other apparatus that transfer pollen to flowers. These tools are employed in agricultural processes for producing new plant varieties or enhancing seed set.
[A01H 1/04] Processes of selection -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Marker-assisted breeding; specific allelic combination. Methods of using phenotypic markers for selection, where the traits are outside of the scope of the output or input traits listed in A01H1/10 – A01H1/129 .
[A01H 1/045] using molecular markers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Marker-assisted breeding using molecular markers described by a specific sequence.
[A01H 1/06] Processes for producing mutations, e.g. treatment with chemicals or with radiation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Altering repair and recombination systems.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Specific mutations prepared by genetic engineering on plant cell or plant tissues -> C12N15/00 - Process for producing transgenic plants -> C12N15/82
[A01H 1/08] Methods for producing changes in chromosome number -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Creation of haploids or doubled haploids.
[A01H 1/09] Apparatus for producing changes in chromosome number -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus or devices specifically adapted for producing plants with changes in chromosome number, e.g. haploids or double haploids, and methods based on the use or operation of the apparatus or its parts.
[A01H 1/10] Processes for modifying non-agronomic quality output traits, e.g. for industrial processing; Value added, non-agronomic traits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of plants with modified non-agronomic traits, for instance traits associated with the production of biomolecules like nicotine or caffeine, or modification of lipid or pigment metabolisms.
[A01H 11/00] Bryophytes, e.g. mosses, liverworts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  New non-transgenic bryophytes, e.g. mosses or liverworts.
[A01H 1/101] involving biosynthetic or metabolic pathways, i.e. metabolic engineering, e.g. nicotine or caffeine -- A01H 1/101 covers plant-producing processes that modify biosynthetic or metabolic pathways, commonly called metabolic engineering, to create or enhance compounds such as nicotine, caffeine or other natural products within the plant. It includes techniques that alter the plant's internal chemistry to obtain desired substances.
[A01H 1/102] involving modified carbohydrate or sugar alcohol metabolism, e.g. starch biosynthesis -- A01H 1/102 covers plant breeding or genetic-engineering processes that modify carbohydrate or sugar-alcohol metabolism, such as altering starch biosynthesis in crops. It is used for methods that produce new plant varieties with changed sugar pathways.
[A01H 1/103] Non-starch polysaccharides, e.g. cellulose, fructans or levans -- The subclass A01H 1/103 covers processes for producing or extracting non-starch polysaccharides such as cellulose, fructans or levans, typically from plant material. It relates to agricultural methods for obtaining these polymeric carbohydrates used in food,
[A01H 1/104] involving modified lipid metabolism, e.g. seed oil composition -- A01H 1/104 covers agricultural processes that alter lipid metabolism to modify the oil composition of seeds, such as engineering plants to produce higher-oleic or nutritionally enhanced oils. It includes techniques for changing fatty-acid pathways during cultivation or post-harvest treatment to achieve desired seed oil traits.
[A01H 1/105] involving altered sterol composition -- A01H 1/105 relates to processes for producing new plants or plant parts in which the sterol composition has been modified, typically by genetic or biochemical means, to influence traits such as growth, disease resistance, or nutritional value.
[A01H 1/106] involving fruit development, senescence or ethylene biosynthesis, e.g. modified tomato ripening or cut flower shelf-life -- A01H 1/106 covers agricultural processes that influence fruit development, senescence or ethylene biosynthesis, including techniques for altering tomato ripening or extending the shelf-life of cut flowers. It falls under the CPC hierarchy Human Necessities → Agriculture → New Plants or Processes for Obtaining Them → Processes.
[A01H 1/107] involving pigment biosynthesis -- This subclass covers processes for producing or enhancing pigments in plants, such as methods to increase anthocyanin, carotenoid, or other color compounds during cultivation or post-harvest treatment
[A01H 1/108] involving amino acid content, e.g. synthetic storage proteins or altering amino acid biosynthesis -- A01H 1/108 covers agricultural processes that modify the amino-acid composition of plants, such as producing synthetic storage proteins or engineering pathways to alter amino-acid biosynthesis. These methods aim to enhance the nutritional value, yield or stress resistance of crops.
[A01H 1/109] involving lignin biosynthesis -- A01H 1/109 covers agricultural processes that modify lignin biosynthesis in plants, including genetic engineering or treatment methods that alter lignin content or composition to improve crop traits, biomass quality, or disease resistance.
[A01H 1/12] Processes for modifying agronomic input traits, e.g. crop yield -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Flower development or morphology, resistance to pathogens, climate, herbicide or hormone-influenced development.
[A01H 1/1205] Abscission; Dehiscence; Senescence -- A01H 1/1205 classifies agricultural processes that manage or induce natural plant shedding, opening, or aging, such as abscission, dehiscence, and senescence. It applies to methods for controlling the separation of plant parts or the programmed death of tissues, typically in the production or handling of new plant varieties.
[A01H 1/121] Plant growth habits -- A01H 1/121 classifies processes that influence the growth habits of plants, covering methods that control or modify characteristics such as height, branching pattern, stem orientation, and overall form. It falls under the broader category of new plants or processes for obtaining them within agricultural technologies.
[A01H 1/1215] Flower development or morphology, e.g. flowering promoting factor [FPF] -- A01H 1/1215 pertains to methods and substances that affect flower development or morphology, such as the application of flowering-promoting factors to regulate or enhance blooming in plants. It falls within the agriculture section for new plants or processes for obtaining them, specifically under processes.
[A01H 1/122] for stress resistance, e.g. heavy metal resistance -- This CPC subclass concerns processes for developing or treating plants to enhance their resistance to environmental stresses such as heavy metal toxicity. It includes methods for selecting, breeding, or genetically modifying plants to improve stress tolerance.
[A01H 1/1225] for drought, cold or salt resistance -- A01H 1/1225 in the Cooperative Patent Classification system pertains to processes for obtaining new plants with enhanced traits, specifically focusing on developing resistance to environmental stresses such as drought, cold, or salt. This classification is used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office to organize patents related to plant breeding and genetic engineering.
[A01H 1/123] for herbicide resistance -- A01H 1/123 covers processes for developing or selecting plant varieties that are resistant to herbicides, enabling their cultivation in fields where weed control chemicals are applied. The entry includes breeding, genetic engineering, and screening techniques aimed at imparting or confirming herbicide tolerance in crops.
[A01H 1/1235] to glyphosate -- A01H 1/1235 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to processes for obtaining new plants that are specifically engineered for resistance or tolerance to the herbicide glyphosate. This classification is used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office to organize patents related to genetically modified crops designed to withstand glyphosate treatment.
[A01H 1/124] to sulfonylurea -- A01H 1/124 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to processes for obtaining new plants that are specifically resistant or tolerant to sulfonylurea herbicides. This classification is used by the European Patent Office and the United States Patent and Trademark Office to organize patents related to agricultural biotechnology.
[A01H 1/1245] for biotic stress resistance, e.g. pathogen, pest or disease resistance -- This CPC subclass covers methods for developing or treating plants to enhance resistance against biotic stresses such as pathogens, insects or diseases, including breeding, genetic engineering and protective treatments.
[A01H 1/125] for bacterial resistance -- A01H 1/125 covers methods and processes aimed at overcoming bacterial resistance in the cultivation of new plant species, including the use of resistant plant varieties, antibacterial agents, or biocontrol techniques to protect crops from pathogenic bacteria.
[A01H 1/1255] for fungal resistance -- A01H 1/1255 relates to methods and treatments that increase or provide fungal disease resistance in plants, encompassing selection, breeding, genetic modification or chemical applications designed to protect crops. This subclass falls under the CPC group for new plants or processes for obtaining them within the agriculture sector.
[A01H 1/126] for virus resistance -- A01H 1/126 covers processes for obtaining plant varieties that are resistant to viral diseases, including genetic modification, selective breeding and treatment methods that introduce or enhance virus-resistance traits, aimed at protecting crops and improving agricultural productivity.
[A01H 1/1265] for nematode resistance -- This classification covers agricultural processes involving new plant varieties or cultivation methods that provide resistance to nemat
[A01H 1/127] for insect resistance -- A01H 1/127 relates to plant breeding or biotechnology processes that confer resistance to insects, including genetic modification, selection or treatment methods that produce new plant varieties capable of withstanding insect attack. It falls under the broader categories of human necessities, agriculture, new plants or processes for obtaining them, and specifically addresses insect-resistant cultivars.
[A01H 1/129] involving hormone-influenced development, e.g. auxin -- This subclass covers agricultural processes that manipulate plant development through the use of growth-regulating hormones such as auxins, including methods for stimulating root formation, shoot elongation, or tissue differentiation. It encompasses techniques that apply, modify or control hormone levels to influence the growth and morphology of new plants.
[A01H 13/00] Algae -- **Definition:** This place covers:  New non-transgenic multicellular algae.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Unicellular algae; Culture media therefor -> C12N1/12
[A01H 15/00] Fungi -- **Definition:** This place covers:  New non-transgenic multicellular fungi or lichens.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Unicellular fungi, culture media for unicellular fungi, compositions comprising unicellular fungi and processes of isolating, maintaining or propagating unicellular fungi -> C12N1/14
[A01H 17/00] Symbiotic or parasitic combinations including one or more new plants, e.g. mycorrhiza -- **Definition:** This place covers:  New non-transgenic symbiotic or parasitic combinations including one or more new plants, e.g. mycorrhiza.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Lichens -> A01H15/00
[A01H 3/00] Processes for modifying phenotypes  -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods for modifying phenotypes without modifying genotypes.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Plant reproduction by tissue culture techniques -> A01H4/00
[A01H 3/02] by controlling duration, wavelength, intensity, or periodicity of illumination -- A01H 3/02 covers agricultural methods that alter plant phenotypes through the manipulation of light exposure, such as adjusting the duration, wavelength, intensity, or timing of illumination to influence growth, development, or yield. This subcategory falls under processes for modifying phenotypes within new plant or process technologies.
[A01H 3/04] by treatment with chemicals -- A01H 3/04 covers processes that modify a plant's phenotype by applying chemical agents, such as growth regulators, nutrients, or protective compounds, during cultivation or post-harvest treatment, to alter characteristics like yield, quality, or stress tolerance.
[A01H 4/00] Plant reproduction by tissue culture techniques -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Tissue culture techniques, apparatus, culture media for plant reproduction; methods for micropropagation; methods for regeneration to complete plants.
[A01H 4/001] Culture apparatus for tissue culture -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus or devices specifically adapted for reproducing or regenerating whole plants by plant tissue culture and methods based on the use or operation of the apparatus or its parts.
[A01H 4/002] Culture media for tissue culture -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Tissue culture media for plant reproduction.
[A01H 4/003] Cutting apparatus specially adapted for tissue culture -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cutting apparatus or devices specifically adapted for plant tissue culture techniques and methods based on the use or operation of the apparatus or its parts.
[A01H 4/005] Methods for micropropagation; Vegetative plant propagation using cell or tissue culture techniques -- The A01H 4/005 entry covers techniques for micropropagation, that is, vegetative plant propagation performed with cell or tissue culture methods. It includes procedures that use explants, callus formation, and regeneration of whole plants from cultured tissues, enabling rapid multiplication of selected cultivars.
[A01H 4/006] Encapsulated embryos for plant reproduction, e.g. artificial seeds -- The A01H 4/006 entry covers methods of reproducing plants by encapsulating embryos, typically creating artificial seeds that can be stored, handled, and planted like true seeds. It relates to tissue-culture techniques that protect the embryo within a protective coating, facilitating germination and growth without the need for conventional seed development.
[A01H 4/008] Methods for regeneration to complete plants -- A01H 4/008 covers techniques used in plant tissue culture to regenerate whole plants from cultured cells, tissues, or organs. It includes procedures that induce the development of shoots, roots and complete plantlets
[A01H 5/00] Products -- **Definition:** This place covers:  New non-transgenic angiosperms further sub-divided according to their plant parts.
[A01H 5/02] Flowers -- A01H 5/02 refers to inventions concerning flowers, including the development, breeding, propagation and cultivation of ornamental flowering plants. It covers novel flower varieties, methods for improving bloom characteristics, and related techniques for production and maintenance of ornamental horticultural products.
[A01H 5/04] Stems -- A01H 5/04 covers inventions concerned with the stems of plants, including their harvesting, handling, storage, transport, and processing for various uses. The category addresses technical measures that improve stem quality, preservation, or suitability for further agricultural or industrial applications.
[A01H 5/06] Roots -- A01H 5/06 covers inventions concerning plant roots, including methods for harvesting, storing, treating, or otherwise processing roots and root-derived products. It falls within the hierarchy of Human Necessities → Agriculture → New Plants or Processes for Obtaining Them → Products, and is used to classify technologies that improve the use or value of roots in agricultural practice.
[A01H 5/08] Fruits -- A01H 5/08 pertains to fruits as agricultural products, encompassing the cultivation, harvesting, and processing of edible fruit crops. It includes both fresh and processed fruit varieties, as well as related seed and propagation material, within the broader context of new plants and methods for obtaining them.
[A01H 5/10] Seeds -- A01H 5/10 is the CPC code for seeds, covering plant seeds as products and their handling, preparation, and preservation. It falls under the hierarchy of Human Necessities → Agriculture → New Plants or Processes for Obtaining Them, specifically addressing the seed stage of plant propagation.
[A01H 5/12] Leaves -- A01H 5/12 relates to plant leaves that are harvested as agricultural products, encompassing their collection, preservation and use in food, feed, medicinal or industrial applications, as well as methods for processing or treating leaves after harvest.

[A01H 7/00] Gymnosperms, e.g. conifers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  New non-transgenic gymnosperms, e.g. conifers.

[A01J] MANUFACTURE OF DAIRY PRODUCTS -- This subclass covers mechanical aspects of dairy product manufacturing, e.g. apparatus for the mechanical treatment of milk or cream for making butter or cheese. This subclass does not cover chemical aspects of dairy product manufacturing, which include apparatus for chemical processes such as concentration, evaporation or drying, and which are covered by subclass A23C . In this subclass non-limiting references (in the sense of paragraph 39 of the Guide to the IPC) may still be displayed in the scheme.

[A01J 11/02] Appliances for preventing or destroying foam -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Preventing boiling-over of milk in kitchen cooking vessels -> A47J27/56
[A01J 11/04] Appliances for aerating or de-aerating milk -- A01J 11/04 covers devices used to introduce air into milk or remove it, such as agitators, frothers, and vacuum systems for dairy processing. These appliances improve texture, stability, and flavor in products like butter, cheese, and whipped cream. The classification is part of the CPC group for treatments applied to milk or cream.
[A01J 11/06] Strainers or filters for milk -- A01J 11/06 covers devices designed to separate solid particles from milk or cream during processing, such as sieves, strainers, or filter assemblies. These components are used in dairy manufacturing to improve product clarity and safety, typically placed in pipelines or tanks.
[A01J 11/08] Holders for strainers or cloth filters -- Holders for strainers or cloth filters are devices that secure filtering elements in dairy processing equipment, enabling efficient separation of curd, whey or other particulates from milk or cream. They are employed during clarification, dewatering or impurity removal steps in the manufacture of dairy products.
[A01J 11/10] Separating milk from cream -- A01J 11/10 covers processes and equipment used to separate cream from milk, including skimming, centrifugation and other mechanical or physical methods that split the milk into a fat-rich cream fraction and skimmed milk.
[A01J 11/12] Appliances for removing cream -- A01J 11/12 classifies appliances that remove cream from milk, including skimmers, centrifugal separators and other devices that separate the fat layer in dairy processing. These machines are used in the manufacture of dairy products to produce skimmed milk or cream fractions.
[A01J 11/14] by raising the level of the milk -- A01J 11/14 covers methods for raising the level of milk, such as concentrating or removing water or other constituents, applied in the treatment of milk or cream during dairy product manufacturing.
[A01J 11/16] Homogenising milk -- The entry A01J 11/16 refers to processes for homogenising milk, i.e., breaking down fat globules to produce a uniform, stable emulsion that prevents cream separation. It falls under Human Necessities → Agriculture → Manufacture of Dairy Products → Treatment of Milk or Cream, describing a key step in dairy processing.

[A01J 15/00] Manufacturing butter -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Apparatus for manufacturing butter.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  The Figure illustrates a butter churn.
[A01J 15/02] Stationary churns with beating equipment -- A01J 15/02 covers stationary churns equipped with beating mechanisms, used in the production of butter as part of dairy manufacturing processes. The classification pertains to fixed churning machines that incorporate agitators to separate butter from cream. This entry falls under the broader categories of human necessities, agriculture, and dairy product manufacturing.
[A01J 15/04] Rotating or oscillating churns -- A01J 15/04 pertains to butter-making equipment that uses rotating or oscillating churns to agitate milk during the manufacturing process. The code classifies inventions involving dairy-processing machines that employ a back-and-forth or spinning motion to improve churning efficiency.
[A01J 15/06] with beating equipment which is movable in respect of the churn wall -- A01J 15/06 relates to butter-making devices that incorporate beating or churning equipment capable of moving relative to the churn wall. The classification covers apparatus where the agitator can shift its position during operation, improving mixing and separation in the manufacture of butter within dairy processing.
[A01J 15/08] without beating equipment which is movable in respect of the churn wall -- A01J 15/08 pertains to the manufacturing of butter using equipment where the beating mechanism does not move relative to the churn wall, focusing on specific designs for efficient butter production.
[A01J 15/10] Devices for manufacturing butter other than by churns -- A01J 15/10 defines devices for producing butter that do not employ conventional churns, including continuous butter-makers, mixers, separators and other mechanical systems that extract fat and form butter by alternative means. The subclass groups equipment that automates or streamlines butter production within the broader field of dairy product manufacturing.
[A01J 15/12] with arrangements for making butter in a continuous process -- A01J 15/12 covers equipment and arrangements for producing butter in a continuous process, falling under the dairy-product manufacturing sector of the CPC hierarchy for agriculture and human necessities. It details mechanisms that automate butter extraction, churning, and separation without batch interruptions.
[A01J 15/14] Churns with arrangements for making butter by blowing-in air -- A01J 15/14 covers churns designed to produce butter by introducing air into the cream during agitation. The classification pertains to dairy-product manufacturing equipment that uses blown-in air to accelerate butter formation, fitting within the broader hierarchy of human necessities, agriculture and dairy processing.
[A01J 15/16] Details -- A01J 15/16 in the Cooperative Patent Classification, used by the European and United States patent offices, pertains to specific details involved in the manufacturing of butter under the broader category of dairy product production. This code covers particular aspects or components of the butter-making process.
[A01J 15/18] Devices for de-aerating -- A01J 15/18 covers equipment that removes air from milk or cream during butter production, improving texture and shelf life. These de-aerating devices are used in dairy processing plants to prevent oxidation and enhance product quality.
[A01J 15/20] Incorporation of revolution counters -- A01J 15/20 covers the integration of revolution counters into butter-making equipment, enabling precise measurement of the number of rotations of churns or mixers during the manufacturing process. This monitoring aids in controlling texture, consistency and processing time in dairy product production.
[A01J 15/22] Lids or covers for butter churns -- A01J 15/22 relates to lids or covers for butter churns, devices that protect the churn contents during butter production. The class falls under manufacture of dairy products in agriculture, focusing on hygienic containment and handling of churned butter.
[A01J 15/24] Beaters for butter churns -- A01J 15/24 covers mechanical beaters used in butter-churning operations, such as paddles, hammers or other agitators that mix and work the curd to develop the desired texture and consistency during dairy processing.
[A01J 15/25] Means for removing butter from churns or the like -- A01J 15/25 covers devices and mechanisms designed to extract butter from churns or similar containers during dairy processing. These include spatulas, knives, levers or automated systems that separate solid butter from buttermilk, facilitating efficient removal and handling in butter
[A01J 15/26] Combined appliances for separating, churning and kneading -- A01J 15/26 covers integrated dairy machines that combine the functions of separating cream, churning it into butter and then kneading the butter to achieve the desired texture. These combined appliances streamline the butter-making process by performing all three operations in a single unit, improving efficiency and consistency in dairy production.
[A01J 15/28] Driving mechanisms -- The A01J 15/28 subclass relates to the driving mechanisms used in butter-manufacturing processes, covering devices such as motors, gear trains, agitators and other power-transmission components that operate churns, mixers or other equipment for converting cream into butter. This falls under the hierarchy of human necessities, agriculture, manufacture of dairy products, and manufacturing butter.

[A01J 25/001] in basins -- This subclass pertains to cheese-making operations carried out in basins, such as the heating, stirring, cutting or draining of curd within a basin vessel. It includes equipment and methods specifically adapted for basin-based processing steps in dairy production.
[A01J 25/002] continuously -- This CPC subclass covers continuous cheese-making processes, including uninterrupted coagulation, curd handling, and whey removal, as well as equipment that enables nonstop production lines.
[A01J 25/004] by filling curd into permanent containers, i.e. for sale of the final product -- The CPC subclass A01J 25/004 covers cheese-making steps where the curd is placed directly into permanent containers such as boxes, tins or bottles that serve as the final retail packaging. It pertains to filling curd for sale rather than using removable molds.

It falls within the hierarchy of human necessities, agriculture, manufacture of dairy products and cheese-making.

This subclass falls within the broader categories of human necessities, agriculture and the manufacture of dairy products.

[A01J 99/00] Subject matter not provided for in other groups of this subclass -- A01J 99/00 is a residual group in the Cooperative Patent Classification that covers inventions concerning the manufacture of dairy products that are not placed in any of the more specific groups of subclass A01J. It serves as a catch-all for novel processes, equipment or formulations in dairy processing that fall outside the defined categories.
[A01K] ANIMAL HUSBANDRY -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Milking; Accessories therefor -> A01J1/00 - A01J9/00 - Shoeing of animals -> A01L - Veterinary instruments, implements, tools or methods -> A61D - Harnesses; Devices used in connection therewith -> B68B  **Glossary:** - angling: a form of fishing using a hook and line. A rod or pole is often additionally used, e.g. for sport fishing. - game: wild mammals or birds hunted for sport or food - honeycomb: a structure of hexagonal wax cells, made by bees to store honey and larvae - poultry: domesticated fowl, such as chickens, ducks, turkeys and geese - stock: animals kept or raised for use or pleasure
[A01K 1/00] Animal husbandry in general, especially cattle-raising -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Stable buildings and structural details therefor.  Shelters for animals.  Shelter and stables that may be transportable.  Partition walls and grids that may be incorporated therein.  Stables and shelters that may comprise various installations, e.g. feeders, computer or registration systems, ventilation systems.  Movable animal enclosures that may be part of stable.  Wildlife homes, self-sustaining wildlife habitat sanctuaries.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[A01K 1/0005] Stable partitions  devices for fastening animals  A01K1/06 ; pasturing enclosures  A01K3/00 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for dividing stable room or limiting animal spaces.  Partitions; grids; box dividers; barriers.  Partitions that may be movable, constructed from different materials or electrified.  Different ways of fastening partitions, e.g. yokes.  Doors, on or in stables or their actuation.  Doors or partitions that may serve for guiding or driving, e.g. crowd gates or for starting rodeos.  (Prefabricated) boxes which can serve as independent quarters, within stables.  Partitions may include ground-borne barriers such as thresholds.  Barriers controlling access to feeders.  Separation possibilities between liquid or solid manure.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  Figure 1 illustrates rearing boxes for animals.  2.  Figure 2 illustrates cattle stall fittings viewed from rear.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Devices for fastening animals -> A01K1/06 - Pasturing equipment, e.g. tethering devices; Grids for preventing cattle from straying; Electrified wire fencing -> A01K3/00
[A01K 1/0011] Cubicle partitions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fences for laterally partitioning or forming cubicle partitions in a stable.  Dividers that may consist of single bent tubes or more partitions, e.g. having a mushroom shape.  Cubicle partitions that may comprise at least one standing pipe, or footless cubicle partitions.  Partitions that may be movable, e.g. by rails.  Gate assemblies for an animal enclosure such as milking parlour.  Box installations which are adjustable between an operative and an inoperative position in or to a stable.  Different ways for coupling fences.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  2.
[A01K 1/0017] Gates, doors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gates that may be used as a fence or have a bumper system.  Gates that may be extendible, e.g. have an adjustable frame.  Non-return gates, e.g. guillotine gates, tailgates.  Sheep control means that may comprise a workstation, a race or a drafting gate.  Doors that may be automatically closing swing doors.  Devices for preventing livestock from passing through an open gate, e.g. battery operated.  Hinge locks for animal gates that may have a releasable hinge mechanism.  Gate assemblies for milking parlours that may be automatic, e.g. through a rotating turnstile.  Illustrative examples of subject matter classified in this place:  1.  2.
[A01K 1/0023] Sorting gates -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sorting gates used for separation of animals:  For example, turnstile-type gates or swing-gates.  Livestock traps or sorters.  Gates may be adjustable, may include an opening, e.g. for pedestrians.  Separating lactating animals in automatic milking installations by means of identification gates, switchable gates, enticement.  Gates may enable selective access to food for feeding cattle, for example, according to a feeding schedule or animal weight or size. Selective access may be provided automatically, e.g. using animal identifiers or sensors.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
